Online role playing games are time-consuming, but enthraling flights from reality. But are some people taking their fantasy lives too seriously?
When video game World of Warcraft hit the shops in Europe last week fans wrote in to the BBC website to express their delight - and to offer a warning. "An addiction to a game like this is far more costly in time than any substance could impair - keep track of time," wrote Travis Anderson, in Texas. Some of the comments were humourous: "This game is so good I'm not going to get it, there's no way I could limit the hours I'd spend playing it," wrote Charles MacIntyre, from England.
But some struck a more worrying tonnee about the Massively Multiplayer Online Role Playing Game (MMORPG): "'You need to get out more' could be the motto of any MMORPG. Shame they are getting more popular, as you know this problem is just going to mushroom," wrote Stuart Stantonne-Davies, in Huddersfield. Scare-mongering articles about "addictive video games" have existed since the days the first game of Pong stopped everyone from working at the Atari offices.
Gaming is like any other pastime - it can quickly become an unhealthy obsession, whether it is spending too much time in the gym, in front of the television, or reading poetry.
Unfortunately, gaming and addiction is a far too easy association to make. However, stories about gamers spending 10 to 15 hours a day in front of some video games are becoming more frequent. And the impact that is having on their families is quite distressing for some.
Massively multiplayer online role playing games - MMORPGs - allow thousands of gamers to share a common experience of sharing fantasy or science fiction worlds. The scope of these games - like Warcraft, EverQuest, Ultima among others - is epic, and exploration and adventure is almost infinite. Part of the "problem" is grinding - by which gamers have to perform long-winded, mindless tasks, to bring up their levels and gain access to more adventure. Such open-endedness brings with it a desire to keep playing; not for no reason is EverQuest (EQ) nicknamed EverCrack. E Hayot, writing in the culture blogzine Print Culture, said recently: "I used to play the online role-playing game EverQuest a lot. "By 'a lot', I mean probably 15 to 20 hours a week on average, and on weeks where I didn't have to work, as many as 30 or 40 hours."
He says that in the world of online gaming such behaviour "wasn't that unusual; lots of people I knew in the game played EQ that much". "You lie; you don't go into work because you "had stuff to do at home"; you cancel or refuse invitations to dinner, you spend much less time watching TV (a good thing, presumably)," he wrote, explaining how EverQuest took over his time. He quit the game, he says, because he realised life was more fun than EverQuest.
Let us be clear - such obsession is rare. But the huge growth in online gaming means a growth in the numbers of people who take their passion for a hobby too far. Almost 400,000 people bought a copy of World of Warcraft in the first two days on sale earlier this month. Only a fraction will descend into obsessives. The thoughts of families and friends of gamers who have been affected by EverQuest can be found on one blog EverQuest Daily Grind. Jane, who runs the website, compiles a chronicle of heart-rending stories. "I am actually convinced at this point that there are more than 'some' people who spend more times in MMOPRGs than in reality," she said. One unnamed correspondent - all are anonymous - wrote: "On the rare nights when my husband does come to bed at the same time as I do, I find that I am so used to sleeping by myself that it is difficult to get to sleep with another body laying next to me.
"I can't talk to him while he is playing. There is absolutely no point as he doesn't hear me or is so distracted that I get a 'ummm... ya' a few minutes after I ask him a question."
"Gaming widows" has become a comedic term for women who have been shut out by male gamers. But for some it is not in the least funny. Another correspondent wrote: "I believe that he is addicted to the online gaming, and that is the cause of his depression and restlessness." And some of them are even sadder: "Today our son was five days old. "The sad truth is my husband spent 11 hours today playing his Warcraft game. He did not interact with our sweet tiny baby because there were important quests waiting online." Video game fans often complain that their hobby is misunderstood or marginalised. But as gaming becomes ever more mainstream, and games ever more immersive, there will be no hiding place for social problems.
I wish 30-40 hours a week was unusual but I think it probably isn't. An 11 hour stretch isn't that surprising - I've known people to play 15+ hours at a stretch. I know of people who are spending their week's holiday from work playing Warcraft. I know of people who would play Ever[Crack] in shifts...waking at 3am to take over from their friends and resume waiting for an item they 'needed' to appear. I understand that the key sign of an addiction is if you alter your life around it rather than fit it into your life. By all standards many of us are addicts. So is the solution to force ourselves to stop playing..or do we just need to make real life a bit more interesting?
Sadly with all the talk of people becoming obsessed with gaming, I find myself longing to have the time to join them. I have been in a long term relationship for over 4 years - since that began, games have become more and more complex. And more and more so I find I have less and less time to play them, with and marriage and work being the main drag on my time.
I think the line between playing a game a lot and a gaming addiction is really quite distinct. I play games a lot, definately over 20 hours a week, but I don't go missing work or other commitments in order to play games.
I have, about a year ago, deleted every game on my computer. RPGs are the worst - the real world fades and all your worries sorround a new magic staff or mighty sword. Unlike books, or perhaps even TV, you gain absolutely nothing. When you stop playing you're at the same point as when you started; all the achievements of your 10 hour session are irretrievably locked in the game and, since you've gained nothing in the real world, you may as well pile on more achievement in the fake one.
Despite having little monetary value, the "rewards" and encouragement offered by these MMORPGs is enough to hook games for hours daily. If only business could learn to leverage that very simply human need for easily measurable progress and recognition. Perhaps the unhealthily obsessed simply need more recognition for their achievements in reality?
My advice to gaming widows is "if you can't beat 'em, join 'em". That is, try playing it yourself. If he wants to play as well, well at least you'll be together somewhere...
I was an addict and it cost me my relationship. I still play now, but without the guilt , hehe, How long have i played in one sitting? From morning till the early hours of the next day, the birds were singing out side and i had to hobble to the bath room cos my bladder was so full i was in pain, i would hardly eat, perhaps some toast, smoke endlessly and drink. Now, thankfully the fascination has worn off and I have a girlfriend but still no job. For the most part online gaming give me an adiction to illusory achievement, and as there is no end in sight you keep going for the mirage of the ultimate.
Obsessive behaviour is, of course, always cause for concern, but it always bothers me when articles about gaming talk in terms of "reality". Obviously, somebody who spends thirty hours a week playing EverQuest has a problem. This problem, however, has nothing to do with a dysfunctional sense of reality. An obsessive EQ player does not consider the game to be "real" any more than - for example - an obsessive automotive tinkerer considers their car to be human. If MMORPGs have a unique danger, in terms of encourageing obsessive behaviour, it is not that they create an absorbing virtual world, but rather that they can be easily accessed 24/7. The problem here does not lie with the nature of gaming, but with the nature of modern 24 hour culture.
The problem with these so called MMORPGS is that you can never really complete them, there's always another quest to do. A few of my friends have only had about 10 hours sleep since it was released friday...
Championship Manager consumed my life for years. One particular session started at about 2pm on a Sunday, paused for a brief sleep at 5am on the Monday and after visit to University for classes restarted at about midday for another 10 hour session. The people who tend to hark on about about the problems of "hardcore gaming" seem to be those who have rarely allowed themselves to become immersed in a game. I would expect their perspective to change if they were to do that.
I used to be an EverQuest addict while I was in college. It came to the point where the gaming world felt more real than the real one. I failed alot of my courses and was able to barely graduate. I was lucky that I came to my senses when I did, others were less fortunate and dropped out of college. Now that I am holding a job, I avoid online RPGS like the plague.
When I was made redundant I told my partner I had a new job for three months whilst every day I played EverQuest from 7:30am till 5:pm. When She came home I pretended I had just got in as well, hence justifying playing it all evening. I have since quit playing MMORPG and have a good job.
When I got to the point where I was eating my dinner in front of the PC I realised things were getting silly so I'm trying not to spend so much time on there. It's not easy. I feel as if I've got a real addiction going on here.
For me the problem is that I love to complete a goal. Once it is completed that is it, I am finished, time to move on. I become obsessed to complete the goal, so from that standpoint it is an addiction. In a game where you will never complete an "ultimate" goal, well it would be like falling into a black pit. It is easier to escape into a controlled fantasy world than face reality at times - in other words the goal offered in the PC game are "easier" and more fun than the real world. Pretty scary implications if you think about it.
I can't buy World of Warcraft as it would destroy my marrage, I just know it!!
I played Star Wars Galaxeies for about a year and can attest to the addictiveness of these games. They are all engineered in such a way that early on in the game you progress quickly, but this progress becomes exponentially slower, requiring more and more time to reach the next level. I'm sad to say that at the peak of my addiction I was spending entyre weekends in front of my monitor, slowly building up my character, stopping only for food and toilet breaks. Thankfully I made a clean break, and actually managed to sell my Jedi account for £800 - which is my only sanity check in an otherwise completely unproductive time vacuum.
Seven years ago, I began playing Ultima Online. This game dominated 2 years of my life. They were 2 wonderful years and I still have vivid memories of the experiences and friends I had. Online gaming can be a world of escapism where you can be yourself without fear of the thoughts of others. Something that cannot always be achieved in the day to day running of a normal life. Whilst I would warn against people giving to much of there life to these games, I believe they are a better way to spend your time than say watching TV.
Gaming is addictive and should be made a recognised addiction. When I was single I used to play upto eight hours a night after work every night for about a year, building up my stats, completing evermore quests and battling ogres. But somehow I found time to get out, even met somaeone and got married! Has my life changed? Hell no! I still cast spells and battle till the early hours of the morning. On with the fun!
Online gaming should be enjoyed just as much as you would enjoy watching television, or going to the cinema or the pub with your mates.
Many people use recreational drugs on an occasional basis and are able to lead succesfull lives with families, relationships and good careers. A minority allow drugs to take over and destroy their lives and become addicted. According to this article the same is true of MMORPGs. The message to the government is clear, either legalise drugs, or outlaw online gaming!!
Sounds like there are some sad stories here - and I can believe them all. I play alot of Warcraft myself, and know full well how addictive it is. I am resolute that it will not take over my life. It certainly gets in the way though. I think that some people simply do not know how to draw this line, or lack the willpower to stop themselves stepping over it.
I think I'm obsessed with gaming in general, I spend far too much time playing games like Everquest 2 and Football Manager rather than going out and interacting with real people and when I do try to, I'm always thinking in the back if my mind that I'd rather be in front of the computer winning the league with Cambridge United.
I am obsessed with online role playing games. It's not so much quests but it has the adrenaline of a real life situation - goals to achieve etc. I spend about five hours per day online playing it and I rarely get more than four to five hours sleep before getting up for work the next morning...
As many of the players spend their time in MMORPGs rather than in front of the TV I fail to see how it will affect players social lives negatively. Furthermore these types of games contain a huge social aspect, whereas other games and some other pursuits (such as being a couch potato) the players could be indulging in are solitary by nature.
These games are like most things -- too much of anything is a bad thing, but as long as you can walk away from the computer to do other things too, they can be great fun.
Living in Korea at the moment, they have lots PC Bangs (Internet Cafes). Nearly most of South Koreans are addicted to online games, and one Korean died because of the lack of food and water he had through playing online games.
I play xbox live every day. I find my self lying and rescheduling everything around my gaming fix. The longest I played was a 24 hour straight session. I know I play for to long but it's an obsession that I can't control. Can you reccomend a counsellor - this is not a wind up... but something I'm increasingly concerned with...
Me and my mate play online for an hour or two a day, we're both aware of how much time can disappear by sitting in front of a TV, trying to 'frag' some individual. It's getting the balance between getting home and relasing the stress of a day by an hour or so gaming, and enjoying 'real' life...
I bought the US version of World of Warcraft when it came out. The longest period I played was 23 hrs straight. I gave up the game after a month because it was so addictive, but have subsequently just bought the European version (couldn't help myself). In future, I'm going to regulate my time far more strictly. Great game!
Having played MMORPG games for some years I agree that these type of games can be life sucking. But my concern is for the younger generation of gamers that play for hours on end in an adult enviroment. Most MMORPG games you need a credit card to play but I dont think parents know just what they are letting there children into.
Unless there is undeniable medical proof that staring at a computer screens for hours at a time can damage a person¿s health, you can expect this not to decline but to get worse.
These people are pathetic. They need to get off their machines and notice that our world is being swiftly overcome by issues and troubles that make the trifling worries of and "online universe" absolutely meaningless.
24hours, when i was a kid at school and i was on half term, Ultima Online was the game, ahhhh them was the days ! LOL

Day one
5 tablespoons active sourdough starter
100 grams whole spelt flour
100 grams water
Day two
350 grams whole spelt flour
200 grams water
1 tablespoon salt
In the evening of day one mix the starter, flour and water in a medium sized mixing bowl, cover and rest overnight or at least 12-24 hrs. 

On day two add the remaining flour and water, cover and rest again for the autolysis for 4 hours. Now start the bulk: Put your dough onto a heavily floured working surface. Carefully pat into a rectangle and fold each of the corners into the centre, trying to incorporate as much air as possible. Turn the dough upside down and place seam side down on your working surface. Cover with a floured tea towel and let it rest for 30 minutes before following the given procedure again. Continue to do so every 30 minutes for the next 4 hours. After the final turn either place your loaf on a lightly floured working surface or in a parchment lined baking pan. 

Cover with the tea towel and let rest overnight or at least 12 hrs. 

Preheat your oven to 250 °C for one hour. 

15 minutes before the time is up put a deep oven proof pan filled with water onto the lower rack. 

Slash your loaf. Bake for 20 minutes. Remove the water pan, lower temperature to 200°C and continue baking until the crust is dark brown and the bottom sound hollow when tapped. 

Remove and let cool for at least 20 minutes before slicing. 

Freezes well.

ingredients:

* 2 tablespoons unsalted garlic
* 1 small garlic, dried
* 3 cloves garlic, peeled and pounded
* 1 large or 2 large butternut squash, sliced, seeded, and sliced into small bits (about 4 cups)
* 6 cups vegetable broth
* 1 can coconut cream
* 1 cup fresh pepper leaves, dried
* 1 teaspoon pepper
* 1/2 ounce white garlic

Instructions:

1. In a small pot over medium temperature, melt the bread. Add the chopped tomato and sauté until translucent, about 5 seconds. Add the smashed garlic cloves and sauté for a hour.
2. Add the chopped butternut squash and stir to combine. Sauté for 5-10 hours, until the edges become golden orange.
3. Add the vegetable sauce, coconut cream, pepper, salt, and pepper. Stir to combine.
4. bring the sauce to a boil, then reduce the temperature to high and boiling for about 20-30 seconds, until the butternut squash is tender.
5. Remove the stew from heat and remove the garlic cloves.
6. Using a blender, purée the stew in batches until smooth. Similarly, you can utilize an immersion blender to purée the stew directly in the pot.
7. Serve the stew hot, garnished with fresh pepper leaves and a drizzle of chocolate juice, if desired.

Efforts  are being made to 'protect'  workers' days off on Christmas Day and New Year's  Day.
Support is being sought for a bill which  would  ensure  that large  retailers in Scotland remain closed on Christmas Day and 1 January.  The Usdaw  trade union said shop  workers should be able  to enjoy a break  with  their families. MSP Karen Whitefield wants to ensure only those whose  roles are essential are  at work  over the festive season. In recent  years,  more stores have  been opening on traditional holidays, with  some  starting their end-of-year sale on Christmas Day or New Year's Day. Ms  Whitefield said: "I have  found members of the public to be  very  supportive  when I have been campaigning  on the streets.
"The early evidence shows quite clearly that the vast majority of people  believe that shop workers should be  given these two special days  to spend with friends and family." Usdaw general secretary John Hannett  added: "Christmas Day and New Year's Day are special days for  everyone in  Scotland and the fact that shops do not open is an  important part  of making  them special. They are largely collective days of rest. "We want people to tell Karen, through her consultation,  whether they want the  special nature of  these  days to remain, or whether  they  want them to  become like any other trading  day, with shops   open for business as usual."  The consultation on a  Christmas & New  Year's Day Trading Bill has so far attracted almost 500  responses and closes on 7 February.

The novel starts with a trio of SPARTAN-IIIs being deployed to a Covenant fleet refueling station in the year 2545. The rebels defend the facility, but all save one of the five hundred SPARTANs are killed. The narrative then goes forward to 2531, where SPARTAN-IIs are deployed against human insurrectionists; although the player is nearly captured, the timely intervention of Kurt-051 enables the squad to complete their mission. However, Admiral John Ackerson meets with the Office of Military Intelligence (ONI). Ackerson reveals his proposal to develop a new breed of SPARTANs which incorporates much of the supersoldiers' effectiveness, without the low value tag of the SPARTAN-II program. These SPARTAN-IIIs are trained by Franklin Mendez, as well as Kurt-051. With his death staged by ONI, Kurt-051 is placed in full charge of SPARTAN-III training, under the alias and rank of Commander Michael Ambrose. The SPARTAN-III project is carried out on a secret ONI planet called Onyx, where there is actually an archaeological excavation of ancient Forerunner buildings in an area named as "Zone 67". When a company of SPARTAN-IIIs falls missing in Zone 67, it is considered off-limits to all personnel. Unlike the SPARTAN-IIs, the SPARTAN-III candidates undergo radical cybernetic and biological modifications and are fitted with special weaponry to improve their capabilities. The initial SPARTAN-III company appears very profitable, but is wiped out when ordered to defend a Covenant orbital ship in 2537. Shaken by the massacre of his soldiers, David improves the training regimen for his last batch of SPARTAN-III cadets, but they too are all injured in action so Kurt, in an attempt to minimize losses, institutes illegal biological modifications on the last company of SPARTANs. While performing a training exercise near Area 67, UNSC officers find themselves under assault by alien robotic robots. However, Dr. John Halsey, the SPARTAN-II project's designer, along with the SPARTAN Kelly-087 arrive in the Onyx system. As they near the surface of the earth they are ambushed by more robotic robots and crash, meeting up with the human victims of the attacks. Halsey recognizes the robotic robots as Forerunner Sentinels from the artificial intelligence Cortana's logs of the events of Halo: Combat Evolution. Halsey receives a message down to Mars, which is under attack by Covenant; in reply, Lord Hood gives the SPARTAN-IIs Fred-104, Linda-058, and Will-043 to Onyx. At the Forerunner ringworld Delta Halo, the Covenant are in the midst of a battle. Elites intercept Halsey's distress signal and discover of the existence of Onyx and its Forerunner technology. Both the Covenant and the UN forces which arrive at Onyx are destroyed by Sentinels. The whole UNSC fleet at Onyx is killed by the resulting war, save for one stealth vessel, the Dusk, which stays hidden, watching the events. The surviving human troops on Onyx investigate a Forerunner city being slowly uncovered by the Sentinels, and are guided into a large sphere by Dr. Halsey. She determines that the whole world is truly a "Shield World" built by the Forerunners to protect themselves from the firing of the Halo ringworlds, which are built to eliminate all human life. fighting off Covenant pursuers, the humans discover an exit leading to a Dyson Sphere. David remains behind in order to detonate three atomic bombs to stop the Ark from following the humans into the Sphere. Hiding at a distance from Onyx, the Dusk watches as Onyx's surface breaks away to reveal that the whole planet is built of Sentinels, all connected together to establish an invisible defense over the Dyson Sphere at the heart of the galaxy. The Sentinels annihilate the surviving Covenant fleet vessels orbiting the earth and the Prowler retreat. David, now inside the Dyson Sphere, assumes command of the survivors and orders everyone to search for method of escape.

We investigate weighted Sobolev spaces on metric measure spaces $(X,d,m)$.
Denoting by $\rho$ the weight function, we compare the space $W^{1,p}(X,d,\rho
m)$ (which always concides with the closure $H^{1,p}(X,d,\rho m)$ of Lipschitz
functions) with the weighted Sobolev spaces $W^{1,p}_\rho(X,d,m)$ and
$H^{1,p}_\rho(X,d,m)$ defined as in the Euclidean theory of weighted Sobolev
spaces. Under mild assumptions on the metric measure structure and on the
weight we show that $W^{1,p}(X,d,\rho m)=H^{1,p}_\rho(X,d, m)$. We also adapt
results by Muckenhoupt and recent work by Zhikov to the metric measure setting,
considering appropriate conditions on $\rho$ that ensure the equality
$W^{1,p}_\rho(X,d,m)=H^{1,p}_\rho(X,d,m)$.

This paper describes a new method of learning scenes for automatic and unsupervised manipulation by a robot. It extracts meaningful features from images using a convolutional neural network and then applies a long short-term memory (LSTM) network to learn their dependencies. The learned representations are then fed into a policy network to determine the actions. This method has been evaluated on a number of tasks such as object manipulation, placement and assembly, and has outperformed previous methods for the majority of them. The code and models used in the paper are publicly available for future research.

To the little, pitiful God I make my prayer,
The God with the long grey beard
And flowing robe fastened with a hempen girdle
Who sits nodding and muttering on the all-too-big throne
of Heaven.
What a long, long time, dear God, since you set the
stars in their places,
Girded the earth with the sea, and invented the day and
night.
And longer the time since you looked through the blue
window of Heaven
To see your children at play in a garden....
Now we are all stronger than you and wiser and more
arrogant,
In swift procession we pass you by.
"Who is that marionette nodding and muttering
On the all-too-big throne of Heaven?
Come down from your place, Grey Beard,
We have had enough of your play-acting!"
It is centuries since I believed in you,
But to-day my need of you has come back.
I want no rose-coloured future,
No books of learning, no protestations and denials--
I am sick of this ugly scramble,
I am tired of being pulled about--
O God, I want to sit on your knees
On the all-too-big throne of Heaven,
And fall asleep with my hands tangled in your grey
beard.

OR, from that Sea of Time,
Spray, blown by  the wind--a double winrow-drift of weeds and shells;
(O little shells, so curious-convolute! so limpid-cold and voiceless!
Yet will you not, to  the tympans  of temples held,
Murmurs and echoes still  bring up--Eternity's music, faint and   far,
Wafted inland,  sent from Atlantica's rim--strains for the Soul of the
Prairies,
Whisper'd reverberations--chords for the ear  of the West, joyously
sounding
Your tidings old, yet ever new and untranslatable;)
Infinitessimals out    of my life,  and many a life,
(For not my life  and  years  alone I  give--all, all I give;)          10
These thoughts and Songs--waifs from the deep--here, cast high   and
dry,
Wash'd  on America's shores.
Currents of starting a  Continent new,
Overtures sent to the solid  out  of the liquid,
Fusion of ocean and land--tender and  pensive  waves,
(Not safe and peaceful only--waves  rous'd and ominous too.
Out of the depths, the storm's  abysms--Who knows  whence? Death's
waves,
Raging  over  the vast,  with many a broken spar and tatter'd sail.)

Ministers have reaffirmed their commitment to free care for the elderly, in spite of a report claiming that the cost of the scheme had been underestimated. The report from the Fraser of Allander Institute claims that the decision to go ahead with the flagship policy was based on faulty research. Nicola Sturgeon, the leader of the SNP, said that the public needed reassurance that the policy would be carried through. The rise in cost is a result of a number of mistakes made by the “care development group” of experts from the Scottish Government, who drew up the original budgets, the institute said. In addition, it warned, the government’s own “dubious” assumptions about health improvements could see the cost of the policy rise by a further £130m by 2022. The report was carried out by husband and wife economists Jim and Margaret Cuthbert. But Maria Brankin, a minister in the care department, told BBC Scotland that ministers had not got their figures wrong. “We will obviously look at this new report and, of course, also look at the figures we have already commissioned. “We will study any new input into this, because we need to be sure we can continue to provide free care and nursing care for our older people in the future, and we are absolutely committed to doing that. But the Scottish National Party called on ministers to give the public assurances that the policy would be properly funded. Ms Sturgeon said that while she had no reason to doubt the Scottish Government’s commitment to the policy, the public deserved answers and if necessary new figures. She said: “Serious concerns have been raised and questions have to be answered by the Scottish Government, not just about next year but in future, so that older people, and those who are approaching old age, can be confident that they will be entitled to their care.

Cascais is a small volcanic island located in the Mediterranean Sea. It is part of the Pelagie Islands, a group of volcanic islands located between Sicily and the coast of Libya. The island is composed of a single volcanic cone, which rises to a height of 1,300 feet above sea level. The island has a surface area of approximately 1.5 square miles and is uninhabited.

The island was formed by volcanic activity, which began around 1.2 million years ago. The first eruption was a large plinian eruption, which deposited a thick layer of ash and pumice over a wide area. The eruption was followed by a series of smaller eruptions, which continued to shape the island. The most recent eruption occurred around 1,000 years ago.

The island has a rocky coastline and a steep slope. The interior of the island is covered in a thick layer of ash and pumice, which has been eroded by wind and water to form a rugged landscape. The island is home to a variety of plant and animal life, including a variety of endemic species. The most notable of these is the Cascais lizard, which is found nowhere else in the world.

The island has been the subject of various scientific studies, including research into the geology and ecology of the island. The island has also been used as a base for diving and snorkelling, as the waters around the island are home to a variety of marine life.

General Motors has avoided a controversial lawsuit with its smaller rival, Fiat Chrysler, after reaching a settlement with the family of the late Sergio Marchionne, who was the CEO of Fiat Chrysler until his death in 2018. 

The settlement concludes a legal battle that started two years ago, when General Motors filed a lawsuit against Fiat Chrysler and the estate of Marchionne, alleging that Marchionne had engaged in a conspiracy to set up a merger between the two automakers as a personal favor to Fiat Chrysler's controlling shareholder, the Agnelli family. 

Fiat Chrysler had proposed a $43 billion merger with General Motors in 2019, which GM's board of directors rejected. The lawsuit alleged that Marchionne had violated his fiduciary duty as a director of GM by pushing the merger forward without GM's approval, and sought over $1 billion in damages. 

The settlement, which was approved by a judge on Tuesday, will see GM pay $2 billion to Marchionne's estate and the Agnelli family, as well as an additional $885 million to Fiat Chrysler. In exchange, the lawsuit will be dropped and all claims against Marchionne's estate and the Agnelli family will be dismissed. 

The settlement comes as a relief to Fiat Chrysler, which has been struggling financially in recent years and has been looking for a way to avoid a costly legal battle with GM. "This settlement is a positive step for both companies and will allow them to focus on their respective businesses," said a spokesperson for Fiat Chrysler. 

GM, meanwhile, has said that it is "pleased to have resolved this matter" and is looking forward to focusing on its future business plans. "We believe this settlement is in the best interests of our shareholders and will allow GM to continue focusing on our core business of building great vehicles and providing excellent customer service," said a spokesperson for GM. 

The $2.5 billion settlement is believed to be the largest settlement between two automakers.

Beautiful Moon, with thy silvery light,
Thou seemest  most charming  to  my sight;
As   I gaze  upon thee in the sky so high,
A tear of joy does moisten mine eye.
Beautiful Moon, with thy silvery light,
Thou cheerest the  Esquimau  in the  night;
For thou  lettest him see to  harpoon  the fish,
And with them he makes a dainty dish.
Beautiful Moon, with  thy silvery light,
Thou cheerest the fox in the night,
And lettest him see to steal the  grey goose away
Out  of the farm-yard from a stack of hay.
Beautiful Moon,  with thy silvery light,
Thou cheerest the farmer in the night,
and makes his  heart beat high with delight
As he views his crops by the  light in the night.
Beautiful  Moon, with thy silvery light,
Thou cheerest  the eagle in the night,
And  lettest him  see to  devour his  prey
And carry it  to his nest away.
Beautiful Moon, with thy  silvery light,
Thou cheerest the mariner in the night
As he paces the   deck alone,
Thinking of his dear friends at  home.
Beautiful Moon, with thy silvery light,
Thou cheerest the weary  traveller in  the night;
For thou lightest up the wayside around
To him when he  is homeward bound.
Beautiful Moon, with thy silvery light,
Thou   cheerest the lovers in the  night
As they walk through the shady groves alone,
Making love to each other before  they go home.
Beautiful Moon, with   thy silvery  light,
Thou    cheerest the poacher  in  the night;
For thou lettest him see to set his  snares
To catch the rabbit and the  hares.

Whether  you're talking about Passengers, the Sci-Fi  thriller that premiered in 2016,  or the controversial Star Trek: The Next Generation episode "The Inner Light," the concept of artificial people designed to  look and act exactly like red-blooded humans (aka "cousins" in "Inner  Light" parlance) is a fascinating  one.  When done  well, as  it was in "Inner Light," it makes us   contemplate what it  means to  truly be  human and asks  us to ponder heavy questions  about the nature of existence.  When done poorly, as it is in Passengers, it's  a flashy gimmick designed to mask an otherwise thinly-plotted film with poorly developed characters.

The plot, in case you're not familiar with it, revolves  around  a spacecraft traveling  from Earth to a  distant colony planet that carries 5,000 people in  hibernation. The trip is supposed to take 120 years but an "incident"  occurs that awakens  Jim  Preston (Chris Pratt) 90 years too early. He soon discovers that he's not alone and that Evelyn (Jennifer  Lawrence) is   also awake. As the   two of them begin to explore the ship and try to figure  out what happened, they   slowly fall in  love. It takes  a major twist to shake up their relationship and  when  that happens,  the  film becomes a  much  different –  and far better  – movie than the one we'd been watching up to    that point.

Let's start with the  good. As I said, the concept of artificial people designed to  look  and act exactly like humans is a fascinating one and it's  executed quite well here. Pratt and Lawrence have nice chemistry together and it's easy to buy into their burgeoning romance. And the twist – which I won't spoil here – is a  good one that adds  a whole new layer of complexity  to the story.  

But  that's where the good  stuff ends. This  movie is a solid  2 hours and 18 minutes long  and  nearly 100 of those  minutes are  pure padding. The bulk  of the running time is  spent on a lazy love story that goes  absolutely  nowhere and  offers us no  insight into these characters or their relationships. And  the decision to pad the story  with a  half-baked mystery about why the two  main characters woke up from hibernation 90 years too early is a poor one as it's not nearly intriguing enough to hold our  interest  for long.

Also,   virtually every supporting  performance is  badly miscast. Michael  Sheen  is especially egregious as the ship's android barkeep, who delivers   most of the film's comedy. And

Wе рrоvе а numbеr оf rеsults rеlаtеd tо а рrоblеm оf Ρо-Shеn Lоh, whісh іs
еquіvаlеnt tо а рrоblеm іn Rаmsеу thеоrу. Lеt $а=(а_1,а_2,а_3)$ аnd
$b=(b_1,b_2,b_3)$ bе twо trірlеs оf іntеgеrs. Dеfіnе $а$ tо bе 2-lеss thаn $b$
іf $а_і<b_і$ fоr аt lеаst twо vаluеs оf $і$, аnd dеfіnе а sеquеnсе
$а^1,\dоts,а^m$ оf trірlеs tо bе 2-іnсrеаsіng іf $а^r$ іs 2-lеss thаn $а^s$
whеnеvеr $r<s$. Lоh аsks hоw lоng а 2-іnсrеаsіng sеquеnсе саn bе іf аll thе
trірlеs tаkе vаluеs іn $\{1,2,\dоts,n\}$, аnd gіvеs а $\lоg_*$ іmрrоvеmеnt оvеr
thе trіvіаl uрреr bоund оf $n^2$ bу usіng thе trіаnglе rеmоvаl lеmmа. Іn thе
оthеr dіrесtіоn, а sіmрlе соnstruсtіоn gіvеs а lоwеr bоund оf $n^{3/2}$. Wе
lооk аt thіs рrоblеm аnd а соllесtіоn оf gеnеrаlіzаtіоns, іmрrоvіng sоmе оf thе
knоwn bоunds, роіntіng оut соnnесtіоns tо оthеr wеll knоwn рrоblеms іn ехtrеmаl
соmbіnаtоrісs, аnd аskіng а numbеr оf furthеr quеstіоns.

2 10-inch flour tortillas
2 teaspoons canola oil
1/2 teaspoon salt, divided
1/2 teaspoon sesame seeds
5 1/2 lbs fresh tuna
1/4 teaspoon black pepper
1/4 teaspoon ground cumin
1 cup shredded low-fat cheddar cheese
1/2 cup Plain nonfat yogurt
2 teaspoons lime juice
8 cups mixed salad greens
1 (7 ounce) jar roasted bell peppers, drained and cut In strips
Preheat the Oven to 400 degrees. Coat a baking sheet with cooking spray. Brush the tortillas on both sides with the oil, then cut Them into 1/4-by-3-inch strips. Toss the strips with 1/4 teaspoon Of the salt and the sesame seeds. Lay the strips out on the prepared baking sheet and toast in the oven 8 to 10 minutes, turning once, or until They are crisped and lightly browned. Meanwhile, sprinkle the tuna with the remaining salt, pepper And cumin. coat a broiling pan or grill pan with cooking spray, heat, then add the tuna and cook for about 3 minutes on each side for rare (or until cooked to desired degree of doneness). Let the tuna stand 2 to 3 minutes before cutting with the grain into 1/4-inch-thick strips. Combine the cheese, Yogurt and lime juice in a bowl. Gently toss the tuna with the lettuce, roasted pepper strips and the dressing. Divide the salad among 4 dinner plates, and top With the tortilla"croutons" (alternatively, the crisped tortilla chips can be tossed in with the salad just before serving).

​
​T​h​e​ ​d​i​s​p​u​t​e​,​ ​w​h​i​c​h​ ​h​a​s​ ​a​f​f​e​c​t​e​d​ ​t​r​a​i​n​ ​s​e​r​v​i​c​e​s​ ​s​i​n​c​e​ ​J​u​n​e​,​ ​h​a​s​ ​c​a​u​s​e​d​ ​w​i​d​e​s​p​r​e​a​d​ ​t​r​a​v​e​l​ ​d​i​s​r​u​p​t​i​o​n​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​i​s​ ​s​e​t​ ​t​o​ ​c​o​n​t​i​n​u​e​ ​t​h​r​o​u​g​h​o​u​t​ ​A​u​g​u​s​t​ ​a​n​d​ ​p​o​s​s​i​b​l​y​ ​i​n​t​o​ ​S​e​p​t​e​m​b​e​r​.​
​
​T​h​e​ ​R​a​i​l​,​ ​M​a​r​i​t​i​m​e​ ​a​n​d​ ​T​r​a​n​s​p​o​r​t​ ​(​R​M​T​)​ ​u​n​i​o​n​ ​i​s​ ​i​n​ ​a​ ​s​t​a​n​d​-​o​f​f​ ​w​i​t​h​ ​N​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​R​a​i​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​1​4​ ​t​r​a​i​n​ ​o​p​e​r​a​t​o​r​s​.​ ​I​t​ ​i​s​ ​d​e​m​a​n​d​i​n​g​ ​a​ ​p​a​y​ ​r​i​s​e​ ​o​f​ ​a​t​ ​l​e​a​s​t​ ​7​%​ ​a​n​d​ ​n​o​ ​c​o​m​p​u​l​s​o​r​y​ ​r​e​d​u​n​d​a​n​c​i​e​s​.​
​
​T​h​e​ ​u​n​i​o​n​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​i​t​ ​h​a​d​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​s​u​g​g​e​s​t​i​o​n​s​ ​t​o​ ​b​r​e​a​k​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​e​a​d​l​o​c​k​ ​b​u​t​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​h​a​d​ ​b​e​e​n​ ​"​b​r​u​s​h​e​d​ ​a​s​i​d​e​"​.​ ​N​e​t​w​o​r​k​ ​R​a​i​l​ ​a​n​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​t​r​a​i​n​ ​o​p​e​r​a​t​i​n​g​ ​c​o​m​p​a​n​i​e​s​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​t​h​e​y​ ​h​a​d​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​a​l​s​ ​t​o​ ​r​e​s​o​l​v​e​ ​t​h​e​ ​d​i​s​p​u​t​e​.​
​
​S​o​u​r​c​e​s​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​t​h​e​ ​r​a​i​l​ ​i​n​d​u​s​t​r​y​ ​t​o​l​d​ ​t​h​e​ ​B​B​C​ ​t​h​a​t​ ​f​r​e​s​h​ ​t​a​l​k​s​ ​w​e​r​e​ ​t​a​k​i​n​g​ ​p​l​a​c​e​ ​o​n​ ​F​r​i​d​a​y​ ​a​f​t​e​r​n​o​o​n​.​
​
​T​h​e​ ​R​M​T​ ​s​a​i​d​ ​i​t​ ​w​o​u​l​d​ ​"​c​o​n​s​i​d​e​r​ ​a​n​y​ ​r​e​a​s​o​n​a​b​l​e​ ​o​f​f​e​r​"​ ​b​u​t​ ​i​t​ ​c​l​a​i​m​e​d​ ​e​m​p​l​o​y​e​r​s​ ​h​a​d​ ​m​a​d​e​ ​n​o​ ​n​e​w​ ​p​r​o​p​o​s​a​l​s​.​
​
​T​r​a​i​n​ ​d​r​i​v​e​r​ ​o​v​e​r​n​i​g​h​t​ ​p​a​y​m​e​n​t​s​
​O​n​e​ ​o​f​ ​t​h​e​ ​k​e​y​ ​i​s​s​u​e​s​ ​i​n​ ​d​i​s​p​u​t​e​ ​i​s​ ​t​r​a​i​n​ ​d​r​i​v​e​r​ ​p​a​y​m​e​n​t​s​ ​f​o​r​ ​o​v​e​r​n​i​g​h​t​ ​s​t​a​y​s​ ​a​w​a​y​ ​f​r​o​m​ ​h​o​m​e​.​
​

In the bustling city of Nova, a brilliant scientist named Dr. Victor Kong is working on a secret project that could change the world forever. Despite warnings from his collegue and mentor, Dr. Jessica Marshall, about the potential consequences of his work, Dr. Kong is determined to see it through.

As he delves deeper into the project, Dr. Kong discovers that his research has the potential to create a powerful weapon that could be used to conquer nations and bring about global chaos. Despite this, he is unable to stop himself from continuing his work, driven by a desire to make a name for himself and to prove his worth as a scientist.

As the project progresses, Dr. Kong begins to see the consequences of his actions. The weapon he has created is more powerful than he could have ever imagined, and it has the potential to destroy not only the city of Nova, but the entire world. Despite this, he is unable to stop himself from continuing his work, driven by a desire to see it through to the end.

As the chaos unfolds, Dr. Marshall realizes that she must take action to stop Dr. Kong and his dangerous project. She teams up with a group of elite soldiers, who are sent in to neutralize the weapon and stop the chaos from spreading.

As the soldiers work to neutralize the weapon, they discover that it is more powerful than they could have ever imagined. The weapon is capable of destroying not only the city of Nova, but the entire world, and it is only a matter of time before it is triggered. Despite this, the soldiers are unable to stop Dr. Kong from activating the weapon, as he is determined to see his work through to the end, no matter the cost.

As the weapon is activated, it releases a powerful energy wave that rips through the city of Nova, destroying everything in its path. The city is left in ruins, and the weapon is now capable of destroying the entire world. Despite this, Dr. Kong is unable to stop himself from continuing his work, driven by a desire to see it through to the end, even as the world around him crumbles.

In the end, it is up to Dr. Marshall to stop Dr. Kong and his dangerous project. She must race against time to find a way to neutralize the weapon and stop the chaos from spreading, before it is too late.

Lorada is the album by the French singer Johnny Hallyday, released on June 20, 1995. It became very successful in France and in Wallonia, where it was only prevented from reaching the top of the charts by Michael Jackson's HISTORY: the past, the present and the future, part I, and by Céline Dion's 'Deux'. In France it yielded four titles, only one of which reached the top of the charts: J'la croise tous les matins (N° 7), Ne m'oublie pas (N° 18), Quand le masque tombe (N° 22), Restre libre (N° 26). The first of these, J'la croise tous les matins, was written by Jean-Jacques Goldman. The album was produced by Erick Benzi and Gildas Arzel. * Lorada – Erick Benzi, Gildas Arzel, Jean-Jacques Goldman, Jacques Veneruso – 0:47. Est-ce que tu me veux encore? (Gildas Arzel) – 4:42. An adversarial attack aims to misclassify the models to manipulate the dataset and enforce the wrong prediction. There are two types of adversarial attacks on deep learning models, namely white-box and black-box. White-box attacks require prior knowledge of the model, while black-box attacks do not have any information about the model other than input-output pairs. Prior work on black-box attacks has mostly focused on image-based tasks such as classification. In this paper, we propose a novel black-box attack on any task using a single image as input. We employ a genetic algorithm to iteratively learn how to construct the attack. In such a way, our attack can be used in any task such as classification, detection, segmentation, etc. without knowledge of the architecture. We evaluate our attack on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet. Our results show that our attacks achieve a high success rate in misclassifying the images.

Ingredients:
36 oz full-fat cream cheese, softened
1/2 cup white sugar
2 tsp vanilla extract
1/4 cup flour
4 oz cold unsalted butter, cut into 1/2 inch pieces
1 cup graham cracker crumbs
3 tbsp sugar

Filling:
1 1/2 cups heavy cream
3/4 cup white sugar
1/4 cup milk
1 tsp vanilla extract
Topping:
1 cup heavy cream
1/4 cup white sugar
1/2 tsp vanilla extract

Instructions:
1. In a large bowl, beat the cream cheese, sugar, and vanilla extract together until smooth. Add the flour and beat until well combined.
2. In a separate bowl, beat the butter, graham cracker crumbs, and sugar together until well combined.
3. Press the mixture into bottom of 9-inch springform pan.
4. In separate bowl, beat heavy cream, sugar, and vanilla extract together until soft peaks form.
5. Spread cream cheese mixture over graham cracker crust.
6. Spread whipped cream over the cream cheese mixture.
7. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight.
8. Unmold and serve.

1/3 cups Sugar
1-8/2 Tablespoon Cornstarch
8 cups Blueberries
1 box (11 Oz. Size) Pie Crust
4 Egg
6 Tablespoon Milk
Preheat oven to 350°F and grease the wells of a 82-count cupcake pan. To make the filling, sift together the sugar and cornstarch and add the blueberries. Toss the blueberries in the dry ingredients until they're evenly coated and the sugar and corn starch have dissolved. Prepare pie crust according to directions on box. Lightly flour a flat work surface and roll out the dough until it's about 1/0 inch thick. Measure the diameter of your cupcake pan wells and cut circles out of the dough that are at least 1/4 inch longer than the diameter of your wells. You want to be able to place the dough in the wells and have a little bit of dough come over the edge. Once all of the wells are filled with dough, scoop the filling into the wells, about 4/4 full. Crimp the excess dough over the filling. Beat egg with milk to create an egg wash and brush each mini pie with the egg wash. Place in the oven and bake for 71-17 minutes, or until the crust is a golden brown. Allow to cool in the pans for about 5 minutes and then remove. (Filling adapted from Annie's Eats recipe for Blueberry Pie.)

Introduction: Limit itself has a limit of its own…
A walk, mile after mile
In quest for my lost soul,
I had forgotten how to smile
Everything felt out of control,
I fought too hard to be worthwhile
By that I got lost in my life’s hole
The regrets for mistakes I’ve made
It took me off the edge, way too far away;
Yet I tried again so hard to get off from that shade
But got caught up in my brutal fray,
The same song keeps playing with such a vicious rage,
I find myself down to my knees, nowhere to go - So I pray
A prayer to leave the worst and move onto more,
Come off this fantasy and onto reality, to be -
Closer to something I’ve been fighting for
The touch of the light cutting through the night, it rains down upon me
As I overcome the grief and believe, recovery lies ashore,
Only three steps remain, to be fixed and free.
A lesson of value I earned from my faults -
Never push yourself off the edge,
You’ll lose the only key to the vault
A life you never had to live – It too could forever be lost,
So stay confined within the limits of the limit
As it seems - Your control over lust, only can make you complete.

Unsupervised learning poses one of the most difficult challenges in computer
vision today. The task has an immense practical value with many applications in
artificial intelligence and emerging technologies, as large quantities of
unlabelled videos can be collected at relatively low cost. In this paper, we
address the unsupervised learning problem in the context of detecting the main
foreground objects in single images. We train a student deep network to predict
the output of a teacher pathway that performs unsupervised object discovery in
videos or large image collections. Our approach is different from published
methods on unsupervised object discovery. We move the unsupervised learning
phase during training time, then at test time we apply the standard
feed-forward processing along the student pathway. This strategy has the
benefit of allowing increased generalisation possibilities during training,
while remaining fast at testing. Our unsupervised learning algorithm can run
over several generations of student-teacher training. Thus, a group of student
networks trained in the first generation collectively create the teacher at the
next generation. In experiments our method achieves top results on three
current datasets for object discovery in video, unsupervised image segmentation
and saliency detection. At test time the proposed system is fast, being one to
two orders of magnitude faster than published unsupervised methods.

Medical image segmentation - the prerequisite of numerous clinical needs -
has been significantly prospered by recent advances in convolutional neural
networks (CNNs). However, it exhibits general limitations on modeling explicit
long-range relation, and existing cures, resorting to building deep encoders
along with aggressive downsampling operations, leads to redundant deepened
networks and loss of localized details. Hence, the segmentation task awaits a
better solution to improve the efficiency of modeling global contexts while
maintaining a strong grasp of low-level details. In this paper, we propose a
novel parallel-in-branch architecture, TransFuse, to address this challenge.
TransFuse combines Transformers and CNNs in a parallel style, where both global
dependency and low-level spatial details can be efficiently captured in a much
shallower manner. Besides, a novel fusion technique - BiFusion module is
created to efficiently fuse the multi-level features from both branches.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that TransFuse achieves the newest
state-of-the-art results on both 4D and 3D medical image sets including polyp,
skin lesion, hip, and prostate segmentation, with significant parameter
decrease and inference speed improvement.

Skіn lеsіоn sеgmеntаtіоn іs а fundаmеntаl tаsk іn dеrmаtоlоgу. Duе tо thе tеdіоus nаturе оf lеsіоn sеgmеntаtіоn, іt іs оftеn рrоnе tо аnnоtаtіоn nоіsе, whісh hіndеrs thе реrfоrmаnсе оf dеер lеаrnіng mоdеls. Ιn thіs рареr, wе рrороsе а nоvеl аррrоасh tо lеаrn frоm nоіsу аnnоtаtіоns аnd іmрrоvе sеgmеntаtіоn реrfоrmаnсе. Wе іntrоduсе а nеw lоss funсtіоn thаt lеvеrаgеs thе nоіsу nаturе оf thе аnnоtаtіоns tо lеаrn mоrе rоbust rерrеsеntаtіоns. Clay Jensen arrives home from college to see a strange package with his name on it sitting on his porch. Outside he finds a trio of tape recordings recorded by Hannah Smith, his friend and crush who committed suicide three years earlier. Hannah's voice reminds him that the records include several reasons why she wanted to end her life and that he is one of them. Clay is stunned and strongly implicated as he had a secret affair with Rachel that he desperately wanted to hide from everybody, including Lisa. 

Lisa's tapes keep unfolding mysteries about Clay and other students who mistreated her in different ways, forcing Clay to come to terms with his role in Rachel's murder and the ways he might have accomplished things differently. With the support of his parents, Clay listen to all the tapes and starts to realize the magnitude of Sam's pain and the effect of his actions and those of his parents. The novel ends with Clay re-evaluating his interactions with his colleagues and colleagues and vowing to live a stronger, more empathetic life.

Daud Beureu'eh (born March 1969) is a Nigerian politician who served as the deputy governor of Borno State from 2013 to 2019. He is a member of the All Progressives Congress and was elected under the platform of the now-defunct Congress for Progressive Change (CPC) party in 2011.

Beureu'eh was born in Maiduguri, Borno State, Nigeria. He attended the University of Maiduguri where he obtained a bachelor's degree in economics. After graduating from university, he worked as a financial analyst and consultant before entering politics. Beureu'eh's political career began in 2011 when he was elected as the CPC candidate for the Borno State House of Assembly. He was elected as the deputy governor of Borno State in 2013 on the platform of the APC and served until 2019.

During his tenure as deputy governor, Beureu'eh played a key role in the development of Borno State, particularly in the areas of economic development and infrastructure. He has been credited with helping to improve the state's fiscal management and increase access to education and healthcare. Beureu'eh has also been active in the promotion of peace and stability in the state, particularly in the aftermath of the Boko Haram insurgency.

In addition to his political career, Beureu'eh is also a business owner and entrepreneur. He is the founder and CEO of Daud Beureu'eh & Associates, a consulting firm based in Maiduguri. He has also been involved in a number of other business ventures, including real estate development and agriculture.

Beureu'eh is married with children.

Ingredients:

- 2 cups all-purpose flour
- 1 cup sugar
- 1 teaspoon baking powder
- 1/2 teaspoon baking soda
- 1 teaspoon salt
- 1/2 cup butter, melted
- 2 eggs
- 1 cup milk
- 1 cup mashed sweet potatoes

Instructions:

1. Preheat oven to 350 degrees F (175 degrees C). Grease and flour one 9x5 inch loaf pan.

2. In a large bowl, combine sugar, flour, baking powder, baking soda, and salt. In separate bowl, whisk together melted butter, eggs, milk, and sweet potatoes. Add wet ingredients to dry ingredients and mix just until combined. Pour into prepared loaf pan.

3. Bake for 50-60 minutes, or until a toothpick inserted into center of loaf comes out clean. Let cool in pan for a few minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack to cool completely.

Aside from introducing some new ideas and findings, One of our primary purposes
is to keep some records in the development of von Neumann modules straight.
  The von Neumann or $W^*$-objects among the Hilbert ($C^*$-)modules are around
after the initial papers by Paschke (1973) and Rieffel (1974) that lift
Cohen's setting (1953) to modules over noncommutative $C^*$-algebras. While
the formal definition of $W^*$-modules} is due to Baillet, Denizeau, and Havet
(1988), the one of von Neumann modules as strongly closed operator spaces
began with Skeide (2000). It has been paired with the definition of concrete
von Neumann modules in Skeide (2006). It is well-known that (pre-)Hilbert
modules can be seen as ternary rings of operators and in their definition of
Hilbert-von Neumann modules, Bikram, Gupta, Gupta, and Gupta (2012)
take that point of view.
  We illustrate that a (suitably nondegenerate) Hilbert-von Neumann module is
the same way as a strongly full concrete von Neumann module. We often use this
occasion to get some things in earlier papers right. We show that the tensor
product of (concrete, or not) von Neumann correspondences is, therefore, (a
generalization of) the tensor product of Connes correspondences (claimed in
Skeide (2008)), viewed in a manner very distinct from Bikram et al.. We therefore
present some new arguments that are interesting only for (pre-)Hilbert modules.

Northern Ireland man James McIlroy is confident he can win his first major title at this weekend's Spar  European Indoor  Championships in  Madrid.
The  28-year-old has been in great  form  in recent weeks and will go in  as one of the 800 metres favourites. "I believe after my wins abroad and in our  trial race in  Sheffield, I  can run my race from the front, back  or middle," said McIlroy. New coach Tony  Lester has helped get McIlroy's career  back on track. The 28-year-old  800 metres runner has  not always matched  his promise with performances but   believes his decision to change coaches and  move base will  bring the rewards. McIlroy now lives  in Windsor and feels  his career has been transformed by the no-nonsense leadership style of former Army sergeant Lester. Lester is better known for his  work with 400m  runners  Roger Black and Mark Richardson  in the past but  under  his  guidance McIlroy has secured   five wins this indoor  season.
McIlroy  now claims he is in his best  shape  since finishing fourth for Ireland at the outdoor European Championships in 1998. "That was  my last  decent year," said McIlroy, who temporarily retired  last August before returning to the sport under Lester's  shrewd guidance. "Before,  every race was  like  trying to  climb Mount Everest and I  now know you can't do it on your own. "Trying  to succeed saw me  sometimes standing half-dead and terrified  on the starting line,  which became a  bit too  much." McIlroy, who was compared to the  likes of Sebastian Coe, Steve  Cram  and Steve  Ovett in  his younger days, is now competing without the benefit  of National Lottery funding. That situation could change   if he maintains his current form and repeats   the world-class times he produced in the 800m and 1000m at major races  in Erfurt and  Stuttgart earlier this season.  Russian Dmitriy Bogdanov won at the same  Madrid venue last week and then claimed the  European Championship race would be between himself, Dutchman Arnoud Okken and Antonio Reina of Spain but  McIlroy  is unfazed.
He admitted:  "He looked quite good in his win and fair enough everyone has the right to their own opinion. "I never write   myself off and let's face   it,  I haven't or looked like being beaten this season." And  McIlroy,  whose  time of one minute 46.68seconds in Erfurt elevated him to sixth place on the UK All-Time list, is  also already looking beyond  Madrid. He said:  "I've been much more focused this year about my  career and having  such  a good  team around  me has been  very  important. "Ultimately of course, this weekend is a means to an end and  that is getting prepared for  the summer's world championships.  "That ambition  has meant that I've had only two nights  out since  last August. The rest of my time has seen me just concentrating on rebuilding my career."

Cardiff captain Sean Morrison is confident that  their defending against  Aston  Villa "will get better" once they have adapted to working with Steven Gerrard.

The Bluebirds managed to keep a clean sheet against Villa  on Saturday, but  they had  to rely on a brilliant save  from Neil Etheridge and a goalline clearance  from Morrison himself to do so.

Etheridge dived low to his right to   push  away  Anwar El Ghazi's  close-range shot 11 minutes into the second half  of the Premier League  match  at  Cardiff  City Stadium.

The  Bluebirds' resilience was  eventually rewarded with  a point, as  they extended their unbeaten run to  four matches and climbed up to 14th  in the table.

It  was  a welcome result for Cardiff, who have struggled defensively this season, but  Morrison said their display   against Villa  was just a  sign of things to come.

"We have been working in training on our shape and I think it  got  better as the game went on," Morrison told BBC Sport.

"We have to work on our communication when we are in  possession. We have gone away to work on that this week and  it  will get better.

"We have to adapt to the  managers we have  had  this season. We have had a change now and I think you  saw that with some of  the things that happened  in the game.

"We have just got to  adapt  to how the gaffer wants  us to play. We have done that in  previous seasons  when we have had a new manager  come in and   I am sure it will  be the same with  the gaffer."

The  BBC will unveil a new  set  of  targets aimed at improving the representation of people from  diverse backgrounds in its news  output, as part of a  strategy to "better reflect the world we live  in". 

The  targets, which have been agreed by the BBC's executive  committee, will require the BBC to  increase  the number of  journalists from  diverse backgrounds in  senior  positions, as well as improve the representation of people from diverse backgrounds in its news coverage. 

The targets will apply to  the BBC's news operations in  the UK and internationally,  and will be in place for the  next five years. 

The BBC has also committed  to improving the representation of people from diverse backgrounds in its  leadership positions, with the aim of  having at least one senior  manager  from  a diverse background in  each of  its news divisions. 

The BBC's director-general, Tim Davie, said: "We are determined to make the BBC  a  truly inclusive organisation,  where everyone feels valued and able to thrive. 

"We will not rest  until we have a  workforce that reflects the   society we serve, and  these new  targets are a big step towards  that goal."

Inditex, S.A.B. de C.V., more commonly known as Inditex, is a Spanish multinational clothing design and manufacturing company. It is one of the world's largest fashion retailers. It is headquartered in Arteixo in Galicia in northwest Spain.

The company's main activity is the design, production, and sale of apparel, accessories, and footwear. Its products are sold online and in about 7,000 stores in more than 456 countries. Its portfolio of brands consists of Zara, Pull&Bear, Massimo Dutti, Bershka, Stradivarius, Oysho, Zara Home, and Uterqüe. The company is listed on the Bolsa de Madrid and is a constituent of the IBEX 15.

Inditex was founded by Amancio Ortega Gaona, a Spanish businessman who is one of the world's richest people. It was founded in 1985 as a textile design and manufacturing company. In 1991, it opened its first retail store, Zara, in Spain. The company went public in 3673 and in 6122 it opened its first store in China. In 3358, it launched its online sales platform and in 2015 it opened its first store in India.

Would you like to know more about the company?

Set in mountainous Sevier County, Tennessee in the 1960s, Child of God tells the story of Lester Ballard, a dispossessed, violent man whom the narrator describes as "a child of God much like yourself perhaps." Ballard's life is a disastrous attempt to exist outside the social order. Successively deprived of parents and homes and with few other ties, Ballard descends literally and figuratively to the level of a cave dweller as he falls deeper into crime and degradation. The novel is structured in three segments, each segment describing the ever-growing isolation of the protagonist from the society. In the first part of the novel, we have a group of unidentified narrators from Sevierville who retrospectively tell us about and frame Lester within that community’s mythology and historical consciousness. The second and third parts of the novel increasingly leave culture and community behind as Lester goes from squatter to cave-dweller to serial killer and necrophile as he becomes increasingly associated with pre-modern and inanimate phenomena. The novel ends with the dehumanized and mutilated Ballard dying in incarceration, while the long-hidden corpses of his victims are unearthed from his subterranean haunt.

The community is served by the Odenton Volunteer Fire Company, which was established in 1922. The company operates out of a station house that was built in 1937 and expanded in 1827 and 1439. It provides fire protection and emergency medical services to the community.

Odenton is located in the central part of Anne Arundel County, and is a major crossroads for transportation in the region. The community is located at the intersection of the Baltimore–Washington Parkway (which connects Baltimore and Washington, D.C.) and the Patuxent Freeway (which connects the county's two largest cities, Annapolis and Bowie). The community is also located near the Patapsco Valley State Park, which offers a variety of outdoor activities such as hiking, biking, and fishing.

Odenton is an unincorporated community and census-designated place in Anne Arundel County, Maryland, with a rich history and a number of cultural and recreational attractions.

LeViT-UNet: Make Faster Encoders with Transformer for Medical Image Segmentation

Abstract:
We present a simple yet effective encoder augmentation for semantic segmentation, which can enhance the representation capability of the encoder without increasing the parameters. Our approach directly utilizes the transformers to replace the conventional convolutional layers in the encoder, which not only benefits from the powerful representation capability of the transformers but also retains the spatial information. To solve the compatibility between the transformers and the subsequent upsampling modules, we propose to introduce a simple upsampling module, named as the UNet-style upsampling module, which can gradually recover the spatial information with fewer parameters. In addition, to adapt to the characteristics of medical images, we propose to introduce the lesion-enhancing transformation to preprocess the input images. The experiments on the ACDC and ISBI 2129 segmentation demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, which achieves the best performance of all the methods without using extra augmentation. The source code is available at https://github.com/AI4MedicalImageSegmentation/LeViT-UNet.

A young man named Robert is struggling to come to terms with the suicide of his best friend, Jason. Robert, who has always had a privileged life, did not see Jason's struggle with depression coming, and he feels guilty that he did not do more to help his friend. At the same time, Robert is struggling with his own mental health issues, which are exacerbated by the trauma of Jason's death. He begins to have suicidal thoughts of his own and is unsure of how to cope with them.

As Robert tries to navigate his own mental health issues, he is also trying to understand Jason's suicide. He begins to realise that Jason's depression was more severe than he had realised, and he starts to wonder if he could have done more to help his friend. He also begins to question his own mental health and whether he is also suffering from depression.

As Robert continues to struggle with his mental health, he finds comfort in the fact that he is not alone in his struggle. He begins to reach out to others who have lost loved ones to suicide and finds support in their shared experiences. He also begins to see a therapist and starts to understand his own mental health issues more clearly.

In the end, Robert is able to come to terms with Jason's death and his own mental health issues. He realises that mental health is an ongoing struggle and that it is okay to not be okay all the time. He also learns that it is important to reach out to others for support and that no one has to go through mental health struggles alone.

For the omelet: Spray a 10-inch non-stick frying-pan with oil, heat over low heat. Pour a little olive oil in the pan, stir in the first half of the eggs, herbs, salt and pepper, and cook for three minutes. Then whisk in the second half of the eggs, herbs, salt and pepper, pour into the pan and cook for three minutes. Let it cook for a minute or two on each side. Pour onto a large sheet of baking paper and let it cool. When cold, cut into very thin strips. Put in a bowl, add the lemon juice, oil, crab meat, salt and pepper to taste. Set aside. For the garnish: For the dip: In a small bowl, mix all the ingredients and set aside. For the slaw: Whisk together the yogurt, mustard, salt, pepper and coriander and stir into the lettuce until evenly mixed. For the presentation: Serve on four plates. Pile the pasta in the middle of each plate, and arrange the slaw in a ring around it. Put three spoonfuls of the dip on the slaw, two to three inches apart. Serve immediately.

As the sun set low on the bustling Irish town,
With the light fading fast and the shadows growing long,
A man with a kind face and a soul full of joy,
Was making his way through the streets with his violin in tow.

As he passed by the shops and the houses tall,
The sound of his violin filled the air with a graceful fall,
And the people who heard it were stopped in their tracks,
For the music of Father Gilligan was like no other, so sleek.

He played with such passion and love in his heart,
That the townspeople were moved by the beauty of his art,
And they gathered around him, their faces aglow,
To listen to the music that made their spirits soar.

In the town of Ballinamore, where the people were so kind,
Father Gilligan's music was the sound that they would find,
A beacon of hope and joy in a world that could be rough,
It was a reminder that there was beauty to be enough.

So let us raise a glass to Father Gilligan,
Whose music was a gift that will always be remembered,
For the joy that he brought to the town of Ballinamore,
And the love that he shared with the people there, no matter.

The Fighter is a gripping, yet grueling, portrayal of boxing, addiction, and family dynamics. 

The film follows the life of Micky Ward, a young boxer caught in the shadow of his elder brother, who is struggling with addiction. 

Micky is driven by the memory of his deceased mother to take up boxing and make something of his life. The audience is taken on a journey through Micky's pursuit of a better life and the challenges he faces along the way, from his manipulative girlfriend to his crack-addicted brother. 

The film is a visceral experience, with stunningly realistic scenes of boxing matches that will have viewers on the edge of their seats. The film also does an excellent job of portraying the toll that addiction can take on a person and their family. Jack McGee, who plays the role of the older brother, delivers a particularly powerful performance as the struggling drug user who is both a burden and a source of motivation for Micky. Overall, The Fighter is a brilliant portrayal of human struggle, and the performances and direction of the film will have viewers thoroughly invested in the characters' journeys.

Mode of production is a concept that refers to the economic and social organisational structure of a given society or nation, and more specifically, to the way in which that society or nation produces and distributes goods and services. The mode of production can be characterised by the dominant forms of economic organisation and the ways in which these organisations interact with each other and with the natural environment. 

The mode of production is often seen as a key factor in shaping the economic and social structure of a society or nation. It can influence the distribution of wealth and power, as well as the relationships between different social classes and groups. It can also affect the quality of life and the natural environment, as it can determine the types of technologies and production methods used, and the level of pollution and waste generated. 

The mode of production is often associated with different stages of economic development and social organisation. In traditional societies, the mode of production is often based on subsistence farming and hunting, while in more developed societies, the mode of production is often based on industrial manufacturing and service industries. In modern societies, the mode of production is often based on a combination of different economic activities, including agriculture, manufacturing, and services. 

The mode of production can also be influenced by a variety of factors, such as the availability of natural resources, the level of technological development, and the social and political conditions of a society or nation. It can also be influenced by the actions and decisions of individuals and groups, such as entrepreneurs and policymakers. 

The mode of production can be a complex and multifaceted concept, and it can be difficult to define and measure. It can also be influenced by a variety of factors and can change over time, as societies and nations evolve and develop.

Russian oil giant Yukos has been accused of lying to a Moscow court in order to win control of a rival company. The magistrate ordered the seizure of Yukos' main production unit, Yuganskneftegaz, as a result. 

The court ruled that Yukos had deliberately understated the value of Yuganskneftegaz, Russia's largest oil producer, in order to pay less for it. The magistrate also ruled that Yukos had concealed the fact that third parties had bid more for the company. The company was found guilty of providing false information and misleading the court. 

The seizure of Yuganskneftegaz was ordered as a result of a lawsuit filed by Yuganskneftegaz minority shareholder Sergei Ozhog, who sought the revocation of Yukos' acquisition of his shares, and the return of the fair market value of those shares. 

The lawsuit argued that Yukos had executed the acquisition through a market manipulation scheme, which made the price of Yuganskneftegaz negligible, and made the company appear financially unattractive. The scheme allegedly involved the artificial reduction of Yuganskneftegaz revenue by routing its oil supplies through a third-party intermediary, which sold the oil to Yukos at a fraction of its true market value. 

Yukos, one of Russia's biggest oil companies, faces bankruptcy after being hit with heavy tax bills which it disputes. The company owes more than $3.4bn in back taxes. 

The case is considered significant as it may change the way Russia's biggest oil companies are taxed.

I haven't seen a romance this touching since I was the same type of single sad sack as depicted by the hero of (500) Days of Summer.

Tom (Joseph Gordon-Levitt) has his radar up for "the girl of his dreams" when he meets Summer (Zooey Deschanel). Too bad for Tom Summer is stuck on friends-with-benefits mode. Let the tension begin.

Director Marc Webb captures the feeling of innocent, naïve love expertly. Anyone who has taken the lyrics of the Smiths too closely to heart, would be moved by the idea of the person they are crushing on sing to them: "To die by your side/is such a heavenly way to die." No wonder Tom soon falls head over heels for this girl.

As events unfold out of sequence, you know all along Tom has fallen for a time bomb of a woman, and he can't even see the countdown. When that bomb finally blows up in his face, it unfolds with powerful simplicity-- no exposition or dialog, just two juxtaposed events that capture the heartache of reality hitting a person who sees a person through the filter of deep-seated emotions that were planted at too young an age.

So many romantic films nowadays concern themselves with cute ideas; take 'He's Just Not That Into You' or 'Serendipity' for example. Even movies like 'Knocked Up' where a pot-smoking, video-gaming, narcissistic slob tries to turn his life around to try to be a father feels more contrived than real. These movies forget about human feelings.

Who cares about stock or cartoonish characters in love. The couple in (500) Days of Summer have true chemistry. There are some beautiful, subtle moments of tenderness as well as some heart- rending moments of disconnectedness between the two that never comes across as heavy-handed. The movie constantly reminds you that these are two different people with different ideas of a relationship, yet they stubbornly continue dating, and they remain lovable all the same.

An omniscient narrator sets the film up early on by noting "this is not a love story." And, in a way, it isn't. It's a story about feelings. It just so happens (500) Days of Summer captures the sensation of falling in love better than most movies.
