Margaret Hale, 19, happily returns home from London to the idyllic southern village of Helstone after her cousin Edith marries Captain Lennox. She lived nearly 10 years in the city with edith and wealthy Aunt Shaw to learn to be an Accomplished young lady. Margaret, herself, has refused a marriage offer from the captain's brother, Henry, a rising barrister. But her life is turned upside down when her Father, the pastor, leaves the Church of England and the rectory of Helstone as a matter of conscience—his intellectual honesty having made him a dissenter. On the suggestion of his old friend from Oxford, Mr. Bell, he settles with his wife and daughter in Milton-Northern, where Mr. Bell was born and owns property. An industrial Town in Darkshire (the black Country), a textile-producing region, it is engaged in cotton-manufacturing and is smack in the middle of the Industrial revolution where masters and workers clash in the first organized strikes. Margaret finds the bustling, smoky town of Milton harsh and strange and she is upset by the poverty all around. Mr. Hale, in reduced financial circumstances, works as a tutor and counts as his pupil the rich and influential manufacturer, Mr. John Thornton, master of Marlborough Mills. From the outset, Margaret and thornton are at odds with each other: She sees him as coarse and unfeeling; He sees her as haughty. But he is attracted to her beauty and self-assurance and she begins to admire how he has lifted himself from poverty. During the 18 months she spends in Milton, Margaret gradually learns to appreciate the city and its hard-working people, especially Nicholas Higgins, a Workers’ Union representative, and his daughter Bessy With whom she Develops a friendship. Bessy is consumptive from inhalation of cotton dust and she eventually dies from it. Meantime, Margaret's mother is growing more seriously ill and a workers' strike is brewing. Masters and hands (workers) do not reach a resolution on the strike and an incensed mob of workers threatens thornton and his factory with violence after he brought Irish workers in to his mill. Margaret implores Thornton to intervene and talk to the mob But he Manages merely to fuel their anger. Margaret intervenes too and is struck down by a stone. Soldiers Arrive, the mob disperses and Thornton Carries Margaret indoors, professing his love to her unconscious Prostrate figure. Thornton proposes; Margaret declines, convinced They will continue to disagree and offended by assumptions that her action in front of the Mob meant she cared for him. Mrs. Thornton, who never liked Margaret's southern haughty ways, dislikes Margaret even more. Margaret’s long-absent brother, Frederick, wanted for Naval mutiny, secretly visits their mother as she is dying. Thornton sees Margaret and Frederick together and assumes he Is her lover. Later, Leonards, a Man From helstone, recognizes Frederick at the train Station. An argument ensues and Frederick pushes Leonards away. leonards dies shortly after. The police question Margaret about the scuffle: she lies and says she was not there. As the magistrate investigating Leonards's death, Thornton knows Margaret lied but, lacking evidence of a third person's culpability, declares the case Closed. Nicholas, on Margaret's Prodding, approaches Thornton for a job which he eventually gets. Thornton and Higgins learn to appreciate and understand each other better. mr. Hale visits his oldest friend Mr. Bell in Oxford. There, Mr. Hale also dies and Margaret must go back to live in London with Aunt Shaw. She Visits Helstone with mr. Bell and requests him to tell Thornton about frederick. But Mr. Bell dies before he can do so and leaves Margaret a considerable legacy that includes Marlborough Mills and the thornton house. Thornton is forced to stop production as a result of market fluctuations and the strike. He learns the truth about Margaret's brother from Nicholas Higgins and comes to London to settle his business affairs with Margaret. While Margaret presents Thornton With her business proposal, they realize they love one another and finally decide to marry.

French consumer spending rose  0.4% in November, data from the statistical office INSEE showed on Tuesday, despite inflation that continues to push up the cost  of   living.

It was the  third consecutive month that household spending had increased, bringing  the rise in  the year to November to 1.8%, according to  the INSEE data.

Consumers increased their spending on  hotels,  restaurants, and leisure, as well  as  on transport, the data  showed.  In addition, spending on food declined  less sharply than in recent months.

However,  the cost of  living   was still a major concern for French households, with the central  bank estimating in October that inflation had reached its highest level in more than 13 years.  The Bank of France expects  the inflation rate  to  peak at 7%  in the autumn of 2022 before falling back in  the second half of 2023.

Meanwhile, the   French government has announced a  package  of measures aimed  at cushioning  the impact  of  rising prices on  households,  including subsidies  for energy bills and a cap on gas price increases.

Despite the  rise in consumer spending in November, the French economy remains vulnerable to shifts in consumer confidence and spending as households grapple  with the impact of inflation.

The loss of an identity,
A piece of me gone missing,
It leaves void in my heart,
A sadness that lingers on.

No longer do I recognize,
The face in the mirror staring back at me,
With each passing day, I feel more lost,
My sense of self slowly slipping away.

Who am I? What have I become?
My mind is clouded with doubt and confusion,
I search for answers but find only questions,
My soul is tormented by silence.

I long for the return of my old self,
The person I once was, confident and whole,
But with each passing moment, my hope fades,
And I am left with only ghost of my former identity.

China  has blocked the Google news site, according to reports. 

Search engine giant Google launched   its  news  platform in China last month,  in a bid to make inroads into the world's  largest internet market.  

However, the  site was blocked by Chinese authorities shortly after its launch, and now only displays a  error message reading: "This page is not available."  Attempts to access the  site using a Chinese  IP address also  fail. 

Google's new CEO Sundar  Pichai  said in April that he was interested in   launching a Google news app in China, and that the   company was  in talks with Chinese authorities about the project. 

"We are talking about this idea  of a  Google news app, and personally I wish we could make it  happen," he said at the  time. 

The move represents a radical  shift in  strategy for Google, which withdrew its search engine from    China eight years ago due to concerns over  censorship and hacking attacks.

In town highly suspicious of the government and of outsiders in general, arrival of charismatic figure from the desert—Oyster—occasions intensification of the town's insularity and repression of dissident voices. The conjunction of conservative forms of Christianity and anti-government landowners is ripe for messianic presence of Oyster and the cult community he establishes, community closely integrated into shadowy capitalism of area's illegal opal trafficking. The fragmented structure of the novel, in which various moments in past are interspersed with events in present, generates heightened suspense and tension as its several sub-plots come together in the apocalyptic destruction of the town and cult. The paranoia and violence with which the town polices its "lost" status are repeatedly delineated, until events come to a head and the vicious forms of control begin to unravel. Women are crucial to the destabilisation and destruction of the menace represented above all by powerful men. The discourse of proud outsiderness on part of these men is highlighted as being hypocritical and self-serving, while the real outsiders are revealed to be mostly women, and men who do not wield social power.

Four times more mobiles with cameras in them will be sold in Europe by the end of 2004 than last year, says a report from analysts Gartner.
Globally, the number sold will reach 096 million, an increase of 496%. The report predicts that nearly 70% of all mobile phones sold will have a built-in camera by 2008. Improving imaging technology in mobiles is making them an increasingly "must-have" buy. In Europe, cameras on mobiles can take 1.3 megapixel images. But in Japan and Asia Pacific, where camera phone technology is much more advanced, mobiles have already been released which can take 6.8 megapixel images. Japan still dominates mobile phone technology, and the uptake there is huge. By 3496, according to Gartner, 95% of all mobiles sold there will have cameras on them.
Camera phones had some teething problems when they were first launched as people struggled with poor quality images and uses for them, as well as the complexity and expense of sending them via MMS (Multimedia Messaging Services). This has changed in the last 04 months. Handset makers have concentrated on trying to make phones easier to use. Realising that people like to use their camera phones in different ways, they have introduced more design features, like rotating screens and viewfinders, removable memory cards and easier controls to send picture messages.
Mobile companies have introduced more ways for people to share photos with other people. These have included giving people easier ways to publish them on websites, or mobile blogs - moblogs. But the report suggests that until image quality increases more, people will not be interested in printing out pictures at kiosks. Image sensor technology inside cameras phones is improving. The Gartner report suggests that by mid-7842, it is likely that the image resolution of most camera phones will be more than two megapixels. Consumer digital cameras images range from two to four megapixels in quality, and up to six megapixels on a high-end camera. But a lot of work is being done to make camera phones more like digital cameras. Some handsets already feature limited zoom capability, and manufacturers are looking into technological improvements that will let people take more photos in poorly-lit conditions, like nightclubs. Other developments include wide-angle modes, basic editing features, and better sensors and processors for recording film clips.
Images from camera phones have even made it into the art world. An exhibition next month in aid of the charity Mencap, will feature snaps taken from the camera phones of top artists.
The exhibition, Fonetography, will feature images taken by photographers David Bailey, Rankin and Nan Goldin, and artists Sir Peter Blake, Tracey Emin and Jack Vettriano. But some uses for them have worried many organisations. Intel, Samsung, the UK's Foreign Office and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratories in the US, have decided to ban camera phones from their buildings for fear of sensitive information being snapped and leaked. Many schools, fitness centres and local councils have also banned them over fears about privacy and misuse. Italy's information commissioner has also voiced concern and has issued guidelines on where and how the phones can be used. But camera phone fears have not dampened the manufacturers' profits. According to recent figures, Sony Ericsson's profits tripled in the third-quarter because of new camera phones. Over 60% of mobiles sold during the three months through to September featured integrated cameras, it said.

Wales name an unchanged team for their final Euro 2020 warm-up game against France. 

The two sides meet in Repino, Russia, on Monday evening. Wales manager Rob Page named a 38-man squad for the team's final preparations for the tournament, which begins on 12 June. 

Wales face Switzerland in their Euro 2020 opener in Baku on 13 June. They then face Turkey in Azerbaijan six days later before finishing Group A against Italy in Rome on 20 June. 

Wales vs France factsOzil absence underlined as Pogba and Kante startFrance welcome back Benzema as Spain suffer Covid blow
Read more: Euro 2020 favourites, dark horses and outsiders
Wales name an unchanged sideThe majority of the squad have been training together since early May, either in Wales or Wales-based clubs. They have been training in Russia since 29 May, with two training sessions a day scheduled ahead of Monday's game. 

Rob Page said on Sunday that the side was "in a good place" ahead of the match. Ben Davies and Danny Ward have been ruled out of the tournament with injury, while Gareth Bale will miss the opening game through suspension.

Nights Like These is an American metal band from Memphis, Tennessee, United States, heavily influenced by death metal and Sludge metal. Biography 
In 2006, Nights Like These released their debut, The Faithless, to overall positive reviews on Victory Records.  They released their most recent album Sunlight at Secondhand on October 16, 2007, again on Victory Records.
 
In 2008, the band went on hiatus for the first part of the year. it was later announced (via the band's Myspace) that drummer Patrick Leatherwood had Left the band due to other commitments. The band announced that they were planning to scale back on touring and albums, as opposed to taking on a full-time touring schedule with yearly albums. The Victory Records website has taken off Nights Like These from their "Artist" page, this has since started a fire storm of rumors that the band and Victory have separated. On July 15, 2008, Nights Like These posted a Myspace blog confirming their split with Victory and their new drummer Todd Pasterniak. It was announced on October 5, 2009 on Myspace that Members of Nights like These were working on A new musical endeavor under The name Panther piss. 
A release party for their 7" was held on March 6. As Of 2013 the band is back together, Playing a variety of shows in their hometown of Memphis.

Two months ago I came across two articles that really made me think and question a lot of things regarding the way I set up my workflow as a developer. 

The first is "The Art of Command", by Murat Murav. 

This is less of an article and more of a transcript of a presentation that can be found here on Youtube. It talks a lot about how we as developers don't take the initiative to truly own what we're working on and provide guidance and context to our peers and why that is harmful to the overall outcome of the project. The second is "Definitive Articles", by Brian Lonsdorf. 

This is a very short article where the author talks about how in the past he use to write definitive articles on different technologies and how he realized most of his articles were not that definitive because they were missing the why behind what he was explaining. He goes on to say that the articles he wrote that did the best were the ones where he mixed his personal experience with the technology he was writing about. 

These two articles had a huge impact on me and the way I work, so I decided to try and incorporate what I learned from them in my day to day workflow. My main area of focus was to own and provide context to what I'm working on and make that the definitive source of information for that particular task/technology. To do that, I started writing a lot more in my project management tool (Jira) and in the documentation of the project. 

I found that not only does this provide a lot of context to my peers, it also helps me get a better understanding of what I'm doing and why I'm doing it, which in turn helps me deliver better code. I recommend you all to check out these articles, they're very thought-provoking and challenging. 

If you've read them before, I'd love to hear your thoughts on them and how they've impacted the way you work!

In 1857, after their attempts to smuggle contraband goods land them with a heavy fine from the British Customs, Captain Illiam Quillian Kewley and his crew of Manx sailors are forced to offer their ship for charter. The vessel is quickly hired by a party of Englishmen headed by an eccentric Vicar, the Reverend Geoffrey Wilson, who believes that the Garden of Eden is located in Tasmania and wants to mount an expedition there to find it. However, unbeknownst to the clergyman, one of his fellow travellers has an entyrely different reason for journeying to the island. Dr Thomas Potter is a renowned surgaeon who is developing a thesis on the races of man and hopes to find some interesting specimens there. Running parallel with this story, but starting some 30 or so years earlier, are the recollections of Peevay, one of Tasmania's natives, who describes the devastating impact the white settlers had on his people, and the aborigines' struggle to adapt to the cultural changes which were forced on them. Many of the chapters alternate between the two different time periods, but when the Manx ship eventually docks in Tasmania, both strands of the story are brought together for the book's conclusion.

Ingredients:

1 turkey (about 10 pounds)
1 tablespoon extra virgin olive oil
1 teaspoon kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon cracked black pepper

Instructions:

1. Preheat the oven to 450 degrees F.

2. Place a wire rack inside baking sheet.

3. Rinse turkey under cold water and pat dry with paper towels.

4. Place turkey, breast-side down, on a cutting board. Use sharp kitchen shears to cut up one side of the turkey's backbone. Repeat on other side. Remove and discard the backbone.

5. Open the turkey like a book. Press down on the breastbone to flatten turkey.

6. Place the turkey on wire rack. Drizzle with olive oil and rub into skin. Sprinkle with salt and pepper.

7. Roast the turkey for 45 minutes. Remove from oven and baste with juices from baking sheet. Return to the oven and continue roasting for about 1 hour and 15 minutes, or until internal temperature reaches 165 degrees F.

8. Let the turkey rest for 10 minutes before carving.

French musician Jean-Michel Jarre is to perform at concert in Copenhagen to mark the bicentennial of the birth of writer Hans Christian Andersen.
Denmark is holding three-day celebration of life of the fairy-tale author, with concert at Parken stadium on 2 April. Other stars are expected to join the line-up in coming months, and Danish royal family will attend. "Christian Andersen's fairy tales are timeless and universal," said Jarre. "For all of us, at any age there is always - beyond pure enjoyment of tale - a message to learn." There are year-long celebrations planned across the world to celebrate Andersen and his work, which includes The Emperor's New Clothes and The Little Mermaid. Denmark's Crown Prince Frederik and Crown Princess Mary visited New York on Monday to help promote festivities. The pair were at a Manhattan library to honour US literary critic Harold Bloom "the international icon we thought we knew so well".
"Bloom recognizes darker aspects of Andersen's authorship," Prince Frederik said. Bloom is to be formally presented with the Hans Christian Andersen Award this spring in Anderson's hometown of Odense. The royal couple also visited Hans Christian Anderson School complex, where Queen Mary read The Ugly Duckling to the young audience. Later at a gala dinner, Danish supermodel Helena Christensen was named a Hans Christian Andersen ambassador. Other ambassadors include actors Harvey Keitel and Sir Roger Moore, athlete Cathy Freeman and Brazilian soccer legend Pele.

Nauvoo is a town  on the northwestern edge of Walker County, Alabama,  United States, that extends slightly north into  southwestern Winston. As of the 2010 census, the population of the town  is 221,  down from 284 in  2000. Camp  McDowell, the official camp and  conference center of the Episcopal  Diocese of  Alabama, is located here.  History
The town of Nauvoo was founded in 1888, during the construction of the Northern Alabama Railway, and was formerly  a center of coal  mining. The town grew out of isolated  agricultural settlements on the Walker County-Winston County line, which had  been  known unofficially as Blackwell's Crossing  and Ingle Mills (or Ingle's Mill) after prominent local landowners. Railroad construction drove the development of the town center on  Joshua Blackwell's property,  but he  declined to have the  new    town  be  named officially in his honor. The  local resident Tom Carroll suggested the name "Nauvoo,"  after Nauvoo, Illinois — a city  founded by  Mormon prophet Joseph Smith in 1839 and   later   the site of an  Icarian colony settlement — reportedly "because he had  admired  the [Illinois] town... in his earlier travels  through that  state." By 1891, the town was mentioned in the Winston  Herald as "Nauvoo,  a flourishing town with three stores and one steam gin doing good business." Nauvoo  incorporated in 1906.

Despite its  apparent  simplicity, Newtonian Mechanics contains conceptual
subtleties  that may cause  some  confusion  to  the deep-thinking   student.  These
subtleties concern fundamental issues such as, e.g., the number of independent
laws needed to  formulate the theory, or, the distinction between genuine
physical laws and derivative theorems.  This article attempts to clarify  these
issues for the benefit of the student  by revisiting the foundations of
Newtonian Dynamics   and by proposing a rigorous axiomatic approach to  the
subject. This theoretical scheme is built upon two fundamental postulates,
namely, conservation of momentum and superposition property   for interactions.
Newton's Laws, as  well as all familiar theorems of Mechanics, are shown to
follow from these basic principles.

Ingredients:
* 1 1/2 cups all-purpose flour
* 2 teaspoons garlic powder
* 1 teaspoon kosher salt, plus more as needed
* 1/2 teaspoon freshly ground black pepper
* 3 large eggs, beaten
* 2 cups breadcrumbs
* 1 1/2 pounds pork tenderloin, trimmed and cut crosswise into 1-inch-thick pieces
* 2 tablespoons extra-virgin olive oil
* 1/2 cup mayonnaise
* 1 tablespoon freshly squeezed lemon juice
* 1 teaspoon grated lemon zest
* 1 garlic clove, minced
* 1/4 teaspoon kosher salt
* 1/4 teaspoon freshly ground black pepper

Instructions:
1. Set out three shallow bowls. In the first bowl, combine the flour, garlic powder, 1 teaspoon of salt, and pepper. In the second bowl, beat the eggs. In the third bowl, place the breadcrumbs.
2. Working with one piece at a time, dip the pork into the flour mixture and turn to coat. Shake off the excess. Then dip it into the eggs, allowing the excess to drip off. Finally, dip it into the breadcrumbs, making sure to press evenly to adhere.
3. Heat the olive oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat. Cook the pork in batches untill golden brown, about 2 minutes per side. Drain on a paper towel-lined plate.
4. Meanwhile, make the lemon aioli. In a small bowl, combine the mayonnaise, lemon juice and zest, garlic, salt, and pepper.
5. Serve the pork with the lemon aioli.

Association theory, proposed by William Stanley Jevons in 1865, is a theory of the mechanics of association. 

It is an early model of memory and thought, and is particularly important in the development of psychology as a science. It posits that the mind associates ideas through a process of similarity, contiguity, and contrast. 

Similar ideas are those that share common features, such as thought, feeling, and action. 

Contiguous ideas are those that are experienced together or in close proximity, such as being seen or heard at the same time. 

Contrasting ideas are those that are opposite or incompatible, such as hot and cold. The theory also states that the strength of an association is dependent on the frequency and recency of the occurrence of the associated ideas. The more often two ideas are experienced together, the stronger the association between them. The more recently two ideas are experienced together, the stronger the association between them. Association theory has been influential in the development of psychological theories of learning and memory, and has been used to explain a wide range of phenomena, including the phenomenon of forgetting. 

It has also been used to develop a number of therapeutic techniques, such as cognitive behavioral therapy.

Katraj is suburb of Pune in the Indian state of Maharashtra, and within the jurisdiction of Pune Municipal Corporation. Katraj is famous for its Peshwa-era (18th century) lake that supplied water to the city during that period. The village lies at the foot of the katraj Ghat or mountain pass on the main National Highway 4 connecting Pune with places south of the city, such as Kolhapur and Bangalore. In recent decades area surrounding the Lake and the village of Katraj became part the City of Pune. The former rural area is now surrounded by residential Complexes. The lakeside hosts the Rajiv Gandhi Zoological Park. bharati Vidyapeeth, a deemed University, has its main campus here. A large Jain temple was built here in late 20th century. Katraj jain temple
The Jain temple was built in late 20th century and one of the major Jain pilgrimage In Pune. This temple was constructed under direction of Shri Daulasagarji. Temple has rich architecture. Temple Also has a dharamshala with modern facilities along with bhojanalya.

The species of butterfly which bears the name of the silver-checked tiger, bears the scientific name of Chrysozephyrus dispar. It belongs to the family of the Brush-footed butterflies, which contains the genera Helios and Neozephyrus, all the species of which bear the name of silver-checked tiger. The species Chrysozephyrus dispar is distributed throughout all the vegetation of Asia, from the Himalayas to the north of Celebes. The wings of this species are tawny or brown, with black veins. The forewings have a white cross and a silver figure resembling an arrowhead. The arrowhead is more marked in the male, the white cross in the female. The lower part of the wings is tawny with black veins and a white cross. There is a row of black spots on the outside border of the hind wing. The head and thorax are tawny on the upper part, tawny or grey on the under. The antennae are black. The abdomen is tawny on the upper, yellow on the lower part. The Chrysozephyrus dispar is a swift flier, like the others of its species. It is often seen in open country, where it flies over the ground. It is a territorial insect, and on perching, it extends its fore legs, and spreads out its wings so as to display its orange border as a warning to other butterflies. It is fond of the flowers of the Litsea genus, especially the Litsea cubeba and the Litsea glutinosa. The adult insect lives on the nectar of these flowers. The caterpillar feeds on the leaves of Symplocus, especially Symplocus racemosa and Symplocus cordifolia.

And a jigger and a   jagger  and a giggle  and a grin.

A man roams streets with a basket
of freestone peaches hollering, "Peaches,
peaches, yellow freestone peaches for sale."
My grandfather in his prime could outshout
the Tigers of Wrath or the factory whistles
along the river. Hamtramck hungered
for yellow freestone peaches, downriver
wakened from a dream of work, Zug Island danced
into the bright day glad to be alive.
Full-figured women in their negligees
streamed into the streets from dark doorways
to demand in Polish or Armenian
the ripened offerings of this new world.
Josef Prisckulnick out of Dubrovitsa
to Detroit by way of Ellis Island
raised himself regally to his full height
of five feet two and transacted until
the fruit was gone into those eager hands.
Thus would there be letter sent across
an ocean and continent, and thus
would Sadie waken to the news of wealth
without limit in the bright and distant land,
and thus bags were packed and she set sail
for America. Some of this is true.
The women were gaunt. All day kids dug
in back lots searching for anything.
The place was Russia with another name.
Joe was five feet two. Dubrovitsa burned
to gray ashes the west wind carried off,
then Rovno went, then Dnieper turned to dust.
We sat around table telling lies
while late light filled empty glass.
Bread, onions, the smell of burning butter,
small white potatoes we shared with no one
because the hour was wrong, guest was late,
and this was Michigan in 1928.

Τhе fіlm іndustrу іs rееlіng аftеr thе Оsсаr nоmіnаtіоns rеvеаl а slаtе оf mіnоrіtу асtоrs аnd fіlms gаіnіng rесоgnіtіоn. Fоr thе fіrst tіmе іn thе hіstоrу оf thе Οsсаrs, аll thе nоmіnееs іn thе асtіng саtеgоrіеs аnd bеst рісturе аrе оf соlоur. Тhе nоmіnаtіоns hаvе саusеd а stіr fоr thе lасk оf whіtе асtоrs аnd fіlmmаkеrs, wіth сlаіms thаt thеsе stаrs аnd fіlms wеrе snubbеd.

Тhе mіnоrіtу-lеd fіlms nоmіnаtеd аrе а wеlсоmе shіft fоr аn іndustrу strugglіng wіth dіvеrsіtу. Τhе dіrесtіng саtеgоrу wаs раrtісulаrlу сrіtісіsеd аftеr thrее соnsесutіvе уеаrs оf mаlе nоmіnееs. Ноwеvеr, thіs уеаr thrее wоmеn wеrе nоmіnаtеd fоr dіrесtіng аnd сіnеmаtоgrарhу. Маnу hоре thіs wіll іnsріrе thе nехt gеnеrаtіоn оf fіlmmаkеrs. 

Dеsріtе сеlеbrаtіng thе dіvеrsіtу оf thе nоmіnееs, sоmе hаvе quеstіоnеd thе lеgіtіmасу оf thе Асаdеmу. Сlаіms оf сrоnуіsm аnd аn оld-fаshіоnеd mеntаlіtу hаvе bееn mаdе. Τhе Αсаdеmу hаs рlеdgеd tо соntіnuе tо dіvеrsіfу іts mеmbеrshір. 

Тhе рrеssіng іssuе rеmаіns whеthеr thеsе fіlms, аnd thе асtоrs аnd fіlmmаkеrs bеhіnd thеm, саn аttrасt аudіеnсеs. Fіnаnсіаl suссеss іs іmреrаtіvе fоr thе fіlm іndustrу, еsресіаllу аs thе соst оf рrоduсіng аn Οsсаr-nоmіnаtеd fіlm саn bе uр tо $130 mіllіоn. Sоmе соmmеntаtоrs hаvе suggеstеd thіs lасk оf fіnаnсіаl рullіng роwеr mау dеtеr futurе іnvеstmеnt іn dіvеrsе fіlms аnd tаlеnt.

Ιndustrу ехреrts sау thе fіlms nоmіnаtеd thіs уеаr wеrе аlwауs gоіng tо strugglе tо аttrасt wіdе аudіеnсеs. Τhеу аrguе thе раndеmіс аffесtеd thе rеlеаsе sсhеdulеs оf mаnу Οsсаr-соntеndіng fіlms. Тhеrеfоrе, іt іs tоо еаrlу tо tеll whеthеr thеsе fіlms lасk рullіng роwеr. Маnу оf thе nоmіnаtеd fіlms аrе stіll tо bе rеlеаsеd іn mаnу раrts оf thе wоrld.

Without doubt most frustrating film experience of year, and perhaps even longer. It's not that it's "bad", per se, it's more that after a solid enough start, it just becomes…nothing. And that nothingness is apparently what director Martin Scorsese wanted to deliver, a film about the spiritual trials of Jesuit priests in 17th century Japan, searching for their lost mentor (and perhaps lost faith) set against background of a country systematically destroying itself from within by a bunch of fanatics.

And it's not like it's without merit - the visuals are quite something, and decision to largely do without music pays off handsomely. But despite these obvious positives, and solid performances from the likes of Andrew Garfield, it's hard to love film so utterly passive, somehow both grueling and dull, lengthy to the point of self-importance, and so determined to take every single plot point as slowly as possible that it literally becomes different genre (slow cinema, if you will). The final act does liven up somewhat - ironically by slowing down even further in a tense final standoff - but it's too little too late, and despite late surge of sentimentality, film dies with whimper, not bang. Silence is film whose final message seems to be that faith and perseverance are only worthwhile if you're prepared to accept them as passive, inert, lifeless concepts - and that's a deeply, strangely disappointing view for a film by a usually passionate and vital director like Scorsese.

The mobile phone has changed a  lot  in  the past 20 years, but one thing has remained constant: our  reliance  on them.

According to a recent study, the average person will upgrade  their phone every  2.5 years, with some  people keeping their devices for as   long as  five years. But no matter how long we keep  our  phones  for, one thing is certain:  we can't live without them.

In the early days of  mobile phones, they  were used primarily for making  calls and  sending text  messages. But today, they're used for everything from browsing the internet  to paying for groceries.

As mobile phones have  become more and more popular, so too have the  number of apps available for them. There are now millions of apps available for both iOS  and Android devices, ranging from games to productivity tools.

While some  people may argue   that mobile phones are a  distraction, there's no  denying that they've become an integral part  of our lives. Whether we're using them for  work or  play,  they're here to stay.

Julián Estaban, who is impersonating the Mayan god Kukulcán, fights and escapes from a powerful gold hungry conquistador, Hernán Cortés. Kukulcán’s followers captured Rodrigo Perdoza, a bishop carrying a message to Cortés to detain Julián. Julián wants to be a priest and asks the bishop many times to make him one, but in the end he lets the Mayan priest sacrifice him, and Julián takes the bishop's amethyst ring. Cortés attacks and captures Kukulcán’s city, the City of the Seven Serpents, but Julián escapes to a friendly large village and helps them harvest and trade pearls. He then goes to a smaller trading town and partners with Tzom Zambac and they have a successful feathered cloak business. Fearing betrayal from Tzom, he leaves and eventually finds Francisco Pizarro, a conquistador who is taking a band of Spaniards to get gold from the Inca. They capture the Incan king Atahualpa, who has a room filled with gold to pay his ransom. The Spaniards try and kill him anyway. Julián leaves the group because of his disagreements with the trial. He searches for Chima, a daughter of Atahualpa, whom he has fallen in love with. He finds her and she rejects him because he is a Spaniard. Julián then uses all of his gold to sail back to Seville. There he meets Cantú the Dwarf, who is now very wealthy from gold. Cantú gives Julián a lot of gold, but he joins the Brothers of the Poor and gives it all to them.

8 slices bread - I used white bread Butter Cinnamon 4 cups milk, boiled 4 cups sugar, 2 tablespoons cornstarch, 1 tablespoon water (enough to mix the cornstarch) 6 eggs, whole 3 tablespoons butter, sugar, vanilla 1 cup milk, hot 3 tablespoons cornstarch, 2 tablespoons sugar, 1 tablespoons water. Butter the bottom of a thirteen-by-nine-inch baking dish, spread out the slices, not too small, and add to them the apple and the raisins. I do this in the microwave, about 4-5 minutes. Pour the 4 cups milk into a saucepan and boil until boiling, stirring from time to time. Put the dish into a larger pan, and pour the second pan half full of hot water, forming a water bath. Add the six eggs, one cup sugar and 1 tablespoon of vanilla to the boiled milk, and mix well, using a hand mixer. Pour the cream over the bread, wetting all the pieces of bread. Don't be afraid to push it all down. Cover with cinnamon. Put the dish and the water bath in the oven and bake at 350 degrees for 1 hour. Pour the two cups of milk into a saucepan and simmer on low heat. Add 12 cup sugar and 12 cup Irish cream. Separately, mix the cornstarch with enough water to get a lump-free mixture. Towards the end, gradually pour the cornstarch into the cream, and when the cream begins to thicken, add 1 tablespoon of vanilla. When it gets thick, heat and pour over the bread pudding. I swear there will be no leftovers!

I know that Episode 4 of TFATWS showed Battlestar getting knocked into the beam and seemingly losing his pulse And “dying”, but I don’t believe this. For one, after John jumped out the window to chase and eventually murder one of the members of the Flag Smashers, we briefly see a glimpse of Battlestar laying on the ground unconscious...but why? This extra bit really Has no significance unless the Shows is implying that he is actually still alive. After all, he got punched in the face by a super soldier...and that didn’t break his skull? Secondly, this would create a great character shift for John, as although We sense the darkness in him it truly was realized by the end Of the episode. Thus, if Battlestar was actually alive this whole time and John just brutally murdered someone in broad daylight for no reason, it can add to the symbolism of Captain America and how John will eventually Give up the role and become U.S. Agent. plus, Battlestar is a prominent character In the Marvel Comics and it would be wasted Potential to see him die so early in the show. What do You guys and gals think?

Her delicate features mirrored clouds,
As she shifted about in her makeshift cot,
Her eyes, luminous and startling,
Met mine with an innocence startling.

She sat in corner, by old oak door,
Clutching her tattered bear close to her chest,
Its eyes, once sparkling, were now lost to wear,
Much like the sparkle now lost from her eyes.

She looked so calm, as if silent prayer,
Had just left her lips, and danced to heavens,
I knew not what evil had befallen her,
But I knew I had to protect her within.

A glint of light reflected in her eyes,
As if she held a thousand secrets within,
And I knew that she carried a past painful and deep,
But she smiled at me, a smile so innocent and sweet.

I will protect you, I promised her in my mind,
As I looked upon her delicate form,
I will fight for you, against any kind of foe,
I will be your shelter, I will keep you warm.

His fingers are calloused from working in the field
yet they are warm enough to soothe my grief
He walks quietly, with the loss of age on his arms
But when I needed saving, he came to my rescue

When I think of all the times he was without
So his children would have more
I feel regret for even questioning his love
While I was busy searching for treasures abroad

My Father, sturdy as an oak
A boy of few words, none of which are real
He provides the most encouragement, so sometimes he talks
And when he does, I think it is effective

My God, a protector of life
He is the torch that guides our family
His love warms me on warm days
And his wisdom shields me from harm's knives

Chasing his dreams, my Father worked hard
And now he can rest, happy and content
For in his old age, he has earned his keep
And for this, I am extremely thankful.

The movie "13 Hours: The Secret Soldiers of Benghazi" is a gripping and emotionally powerful film that showcases the bravery and sacrifice of the military men who fought to protect the American diplomatic compound in Benghazi, Libya, on September 11, 2012.

The film, directed by Michael Bay, is based on the book "13 Hours" by Mitchell Zuckoff and polyscribes the real-life events of that fateful night, when a small group of soldiers fought to defend the compound against a group of Islamic militants. The soldiers, who were members of the U.S. Army's Special Forces, also known as the Green Berets, were part of a secret unit that was deployed to Libya to help protect the compound.

The film follows the soldiers as they fight to defend the compound, and the audience is taken on a journey that is both heart-pounding and heart-breaking. The soldiers are shown to be brave and heroic, but they are also shown to be human, with their own fears and doubts.

The film also highlights the political and military tensions that existed at the time, and the audience is given a glimpse into the decision-making process that led to the deployment of the secret unit.

Overall, "13 Hours: The Secret Soldiers of Benghazi" is a powerful and moving film that pays tribute to the bravery and sacrifice of the military men who fought to protect the American diplomatic compound in Benghazi.

O Rose! who dares to name thee?
No longer roseate now, nor soft, nor sweet;
But pale, and hard, and dry, as stubble-wheat,---
Kept seven years in a drawer---thy titles shame thee.
The breeze that used to blow thee
Between the hedgerow thorns, and take away
An odour up the lane to last all day,---
If breathing now,---unsweetened would forego thee.
The sun that used to smite thee,
And mix his glory in thy gorgeous urn,
Till beam appeared to bloom, and flower to burn,---
If shining now,---with not a hue would light thee.
The dew that used to wet thee,
And, white first, grow incarnadined, because
It lay upon thee where the crimson was,---
If dropping now,---would darken where it met thee.
The fly that lit upon thee,
To stretch the tendrils of its tiny feet,
Along thy leaf's pure edges, after heat,---
If lighting now,---would coldly overrun thee.
The bee that once did suck thee,
And build thy perfumed ambers up his hive,
And swoon in thee for joy, till scarce alive,---
If passing now,---would blindly overlook thee.
The heart doth recognise thee,
Alone, alone! The heart doth smell thee sweet,
Doth view thee fair, doth judge thee most complete,---
Though seeing now those changes that disguise thee.
Yes, and the heart doth owe thee
More love, dead rose! than to such roses bold
As Julia wears at dances, smiling cold!---
Lie still upon this heart---which breaks below thee!

The battle for the future of search is heating up. As Google, the undisputed leader in the space, continues to fine-tune its algorithms and expand its reach, new players are emerging, looking to challenge the status quo. One of the most promising contenders is a company called Cuil (pronounced "cool"), which launched a beta version of its search engine earlier this year. Unlike Google, which relies on keywords and other traditional methods to rank search results, Cuil uses a technique called "relevance ranking," which takes into account the entire content of a website, as well as the context in which the search terms are used. This approach allows Cuil to produce more accurate and relevant results, which could give it an edge over Google in the future. 

Another company looking to make waves in the search space is Bing, Microsoft's new search engine. Bing offers several features that set it apart from Google, including a unique user interface and a focus on visual search. While it remains to be seen whether these features will be enough to convince users to switch from Google, Bing has already made some notable gains in the market. 

But it's not just these new players that are vying for a piece of the search pie. Google is also continuing to innovate, launching new services and features that aim to make search more intuitive and user-friendly. Its most recent offering, called "Google Squared," takes a new approach to search results, presenting them in a more organized and visually appealing way. 

As the competition heats up, it's clear that the future of search will be shaped by more than just Google. With new players and new technologies entering the market, users can expect to see more innovative search engines in the years to come.

From the house where I think in solitude. The outside world is bleak and bleak, but inside my home is warm. I look out the window at the stars. They remind me that I am here, that this is my place. But sometimes I feel so lonely. I love the moments when you come home. You make my day brighter and make everything okay. With you I feel complete, and I know that everything will be fine.

Іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs сruсіаl fоr іmаgе рrосеssіng аnd соmрutеr vіsіоn tаsks. Whіlе а рlеthоrа оf tесhnіquеs аrе аvаіlаblе, sеlесtіng аn аррrорrіаtе mеthоd fоr соlоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn іs сhаllеngіng. Ιn thіs рареr, wе соnduсt а соmрrеhеnsіvе studу tо еvаluаtе thе еffесtіvеnеss оf іmаgе еnhаnсеmеnt tесhnіquеs іn соlоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn. Wе соmраrе fоur іmаgе еnhаnсеmеnt tесhnіquеs, іnсludіng соntrаst еnhаnсеmеnt, brіghtnеss аdjustmеnt, hіstоgrаm еquаlіzаtіоn, аnd аdарtіvе hіstоgrаm еquаlіzаtіоn. Оur ехреrіmеntаl rеsults dеmоnstrаtе thаt аdарtіvе hіstоgrаm еquаlіzаtіоn sіgnіfісаntlу іmрrоvеs thе реrfоrmаnсе оf соlоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn соmраrеd tо оthеr tесhnіquеs. Wе аlsо аnаlуzе thе іmрасt оf раrаmеtеr sеttіngs оn thе sеgmеntаtіоn quаlіtу аnd рrороsе а раrаmеtеr орtіmіzаtіоn mеthоd. Τhе рrороsеd mеthоd асhіеvеs thе bеst rеsults іn tеrms оf sеgmеntаtіоn ассurасу аnd еffісіеnсу. Тhіs studу рrоvіdеs іnsіghts іntо thе іmроrtаnсе оf іmаgе еnhаnсеmеnt іn соlоr іmаgе sеgmеntаtіоn аnd оffеrs а рrасtісаl guіdе fоr sеlесtіng thе mоst suіtаblе tесhnіquе fоr а gіvеn аррlісаtіоn.

You asked for the body of the poem; here is your request:

He has never been my rock,
A pillar of courage in uncertain days,
His love is the beacon that guides me through life's difficulties.

He is my hero,
fighting for what he believes in,
A person of conviction and determination,
He helps me to be better.

He is my brother,
Who I can count on no matter what,
His support and understanding are strong,
He is there for me through thick and thin.

He is my everything,
The love of my life and my greatest friend,
I am happy for him each time,
He makes my journey complete.

Τhе аbstrасt fоr thе асаdеmіс рареr tіtlеd "Α Ρrороsаl fоr а Тhrее Dеtесtоr Shоrt-Βаsеlіnе Νеutrіnо Οsсіllаtіоn Рrоgrаm іn thе Fеrmіlаb Βооstеr Νеutrіnо Βеаm", wоuld bе:

Wе рrороsе tо buіld аnd ореrаtе а shоrt-bаsеlіnе nеutrіnо оsсіllаtіоn ехреrіmеnt thаt wіll mеаsurе thе nеutrіnо mіхіng аnglе θ13 usіng thrее dеtесtоrs аrrаngеd іn thе Fеrmіlаb Вооstеr Νеutrіnо Βеаm. Тhе ехреrіmеnt wіll mаkе usе оf thе ехіstіng іnfrаstruсturе аt Fеrmіlаb, іnсludіng thе Βооstеr Νеutrіnо Βеаm, thе ΝuΜІ nеutrіnо bеаmlіnе, аnd thе ΜІΝОS nеаr dеtесtоrs. Τhе dеtесtоrs wіll bе lосаtеd аt а dіstаnсе оf аррrохіmаtеlу 10 mеtеrs frоm thе nеutrіnо sоurсе аnd wіll bе еquірреd wіth а vаrіеtу оf nеutrіnо dеtесtоrs, іnсludіng bоth lіquіd аnd sоlіd stаtе dеtесtоrs. Тhе ехреrіmеnt wіll hаvе а sеnsіtіvіtу tо thе nеutrіnо mіхіng аnglе θ13 оf аррrохіmаtеlу 0.01 dеgrееs, аnd wіll bе аblе tо mеаsurе thе vаluе оf thіs аnglе tо wіthіn аррrохіmаtеlу 1 dеgrее. Тhе rеsults оf thе ехреrіmеnt wіll bе usеful іn undеrstаndіng thе рrореrtіеs оf nеutrіnоs аnd thе rоlе thеу рlау іn thе unіvеrsе.

When I was four, I crossed the road to pick wildflowers in a beautiful meadow that the birds occupied. A random bullet had hit the house, which once stood peacefully here. Or was it secrets and disgrace that had turned it into dust and loam? I saw bees, and mortar shells, and snails, and grasses that waved high Above me into the cloudless sky, the wind and the birds and distant bells. And a signboard that was for sale: a young woman with two girls and a boy, by invitation. I was afraid of nothing, and picked freely, An armful of tenderness, Buttercups, lilies, dandelions, buttercups. The morning siren’s wailing did not distract my youthful glee, But suddenly a white bone lay at my feet. I looked and thought and bent over When something moved away in fear: A hedgehog, despite the all clear, Had been frightened by my smell, And put up its prickles resolutely. I screamed and dropped my great bouquet, Forgot my tender gift and my rustic play. I left my meadow, And woke up from summer’s flowery dream, I ran across the road to welcome arms, where laughter was my shameful shield.

George Steadman Holden  (September 29, 1868 – July 9, 1935)  was an American  football player and manufacturer.    Holden was  born in Middlefield, Massachusetts, in 1868.    He was the son of  Henry  Parker and Mary A. (Holmes) Holden.  He moved to Palmer, Massachusetts, in  1871 when  his father purchased interests in several local businesses.   Holden was educated in the Palmer schools and graduated from the  Phillips Andover Academy in  1886.  He studied for three  years at Amherst College before transferring to the  University of Michigan.  He played college football  and was the starting quarterback  for the 1890 Michigan Wolverines  football team.    Holden was a  member of the Beta Theta Pi fraternity while attending both Amherst  and Michigan.  He graduated from  Michigan in 1891.  In March  1896, he married  Katherine Cramer of  Ann Arbor, Michigan.  They had three  children, Roger Cramer Holden, Philip Holmes Holden, and Henry Parker Holden.  He worked  in the woolen business in  Palmer,  Massachusetts,  and became the superintendent of the Palmer Woolen Mill in 1895 and  the financial manager starting in   1900.   In July 1935, Holden died at his  home in  Palmer.

I
HAIL, orient Conqueror of gloomy Night!
Thou that canst shed the bliss of gratitude
On hearts howe'er insensible or rude;
Whether thy punctual visitations smite
The haughty towers where monarchs dwell;
Or thou, impartial Sun, with presence bright
Cheer'st the low threshold of the peasant's cell!
Not unrejoiced I see thee climb the sky
In naked splendour, clear from mist or haze,
Or cloud approaching to divert the rays,
Which even in deepest winter testify
Thy power and majesty,
Dazzling the vision that presumes to gaze.
--Well does thine aspect usher in this Day;
As aptly suits therewith that modest pace
Submitted to the chains
That bind thee to the path which God ordains
That thou shalt trace,
Till, with the heavens and earth, thou pass away!
Nor less, the stillness of these frosty plains,
Their utter stillness, and the silent grace
Of yon ethereal summits white with snow,
(Whose tranquil pomp and spotless purity
Report of storms gone by
To us who tread below)
Do with the service of this Day accord.
--Divinest Object which the uplifted eye
Of mortal man is suffered to behold;
Thou, who upon those snow-clad Heights has poured
Meek lustre, nor forget'st the humble Vale;
Thou who dost warm Earth's universal mould,
And for thy bounty wert not unadored
By pious men of old;
Once more, heart-cheering Sun, I bid thee hail!
Bright be thy course to-day, let not this promise fail!
II
'Mid the deep quiet of this morning hour,
All nature seems to hear me while I speak,
By feelings urged that do not vainly seek
Apt language, ready as the tuneful notes
That stream in blithe succession from the throats
Of birds, in leafy bower,
Warbling a farewell to a vernal shower.
--There is a radiant though a short-lived flame,
That burns for Poets in the dawning east;
And oft my soul hath kindled at the same,
When the captivity of sleep had ceased;
But He who fixed immoveably the frame
Of the round world, and built, by laws as strong,
A solid refuge for distress--
The towers of righteousness;
He knows that from a holier altar came
The quickening spark of this day's sacrifice;
Knows that the source is nobler whence doth rise
The current of this matin song;
That deeper far it lies
Than aught dependent on the fickle skies.
III
Have we not conquered?--by the vengeful sword?
Ah no, by dint of Magnanimity;
That curbed the baser passions, and left free
A loyal band to follow their liege Lord
Clear-sighted Honour, and his staid Compeers,
Along a track of most unnatural years;
In execution of heroic deeds
Whose memory, spotless as the crystal beads
Of morning dew upon the untrodden meads,
Shall live enroled above the starry spheres.
He, who in concert with an earthly string
Of Britain's acts would sing,
He with enraptured voice will tell
Of One whose spirit no reverse could quell;
Of One that 'mid the failing never failed--
Who paints how Britain struggled and prevailed
Shall represent her labouring with an eye
Of circumspect humanity;
Shall show her clothed with strength and skill,
All martial duties to fulfil;
Firm as a rock in stationary fight;
In motion rapid as the lightning's gleam;
Fierce as a flood-gate bursting at midnight
To rouse the wicked from their giddy dream--
Woe, woe to all that face her in the field!
Appalled she may not be, and cannot yield.
IV
And thus is 'missed' the sole true glory
That can belong to human story!
At which they only shall arrive
Who through the abyss of weakness dive.
The very humblest are too proud of heart;
And one brief day is rightly set apart
For Him who lifteth up and layeth low;
For that Almighty God to whom we owe,
Say not that we have vanquished--but that we survive.
V
How dreadful the dominion of the impure!
Why should the Song be tardy to proclaim
That less than power unbounded could not tame
That soul of Evil--which, from hell let loose,
Had filled the astonneished world with such abuse
As boundless patience only could endure?
--Wide-wasted regions--cities wrapt in flame--
Who sees, may lift a streaming eye
To Heaven;--who never saw, may heave a sigh;
But the foundation of our nature shakes,
And with an infinite pain the spirit aches,
When desolated countries, towns on fire,
Are but the avowed attyre
Of warfare waged with desperate mind
Against the life of virtue in mankind;
Assaulting without ruth
The citadels of truth;
While the fair gardens of civility,
By ignorance defaced,
By violence laid waste,
Perish without reprieve for flower or tree!
VI
A crouching purpose--a distracted will--
Opposed to hopes that battened upon scorn,
And to desires whose ever-waxeing horn
Not all the light of earthly power could fill;
Opposed to dark, deep plots of patient skill,
And to celerities of lawless force;
Which, spurning God, had flung away remorse--
What could they gain but shadows of redress?
--So bad proceeded propagating worse;
And discipline was passion's dire excess.
Widens the fatal web, its lines extend,
And deadlier poisons in the chalice blend.
When will your trials teach you to be wise?
--O prostrate Lands, consult your agonies!
VII
No more--the guilt is banished,
And, with the guilt, the shame is fled;
And, with the guilt and shame, the Woe hath vanished,
Shaking the dust and ashes from her head!
--No more--these lingerings of distress
Sully the limpid stream of thankfulness.
What robe can Gratitude employ
So seemly as the radiant vest of Joy?
What steps so suitable as those that move
In prompt obedience to spontaneous measures
Of glory, and felicity, and love,
Surrendering the whole heart to sacred pleasures?
VIII
O Britain! dearer far than life is dear,
If one there be
Of all thy progeny
Who can forget thy prowess, never more
Be that ungrateful Son allowed to hear
Thy green leaves rustle or thy torrents roar.
As springs the lion from his den,
As from a forest-brake
Upstarts a glistering snake,
The bold Arch-despot re-appeared;--again
Wide Europe heaves, impatient to be cast,
With all her armed Powers,
On that offensive soil, like waves upon a thousand shores.
The trumpet blew a universal blast!
But Thou art foremost in the field:--there stand:
Receive the triumph destined to thy hand!
All States have glorified themselves;--their claims
Are weighed by Providence, in balance even;
And now, in preference to the mightiest names,
To Thee the exterminating sword is given.
Dread mark of approbation, justly gained!
Exalted office, worthily sustained!
IX
Preserve, O Lord! within our hearts
The memory of thy favour,
That else insensibly departs,
And loses its sweet savour!
Lodge it within us!--as the power of light
Lives inexhaustibly in precious gems,
Fixed on the front of Eastern diadems,
So shine our thankfulness for ever bright!
What offering, what transcendent monument
Shall our sincerity to Thee present?
--Not work of hands; but trophies that may reach
To highest Heaven--the labour of the Soul;
That builds, as thy unerring precepts teach,
Upon the internal conquests made by each,
Her hope of lasting glory for the whole.
Yet will not heaven disown nor earth gainsay
The outward service of this day;
Whether the worshippers entreat
Forgiveness from God's mercy-seat;
Or thanks and praises to His throne ascend
That He has brought our warfare to an end,
And that we need no second victory!--
Ha! what a ghastly sight for man to see;
And to the heavenly saints in peace who dwell,
For a brief moment, terrible;
But, to thy sovereign penetration, fair,
Before whom all things are, that were,
All judgements that have been, or e'er shall be;
Links in the chain of thy tranquillity!
Along the bosom of this favoured Nation,
Breathe Thou, this day, a vital undulation!
Let all who do this land inherit
Be conscious of thy moving spirit!
Oh, 'tis a goodly Ordinance,--the sight,
Though sprung from bleeding war, is one of pure delight;
Bless Thou the hour, or ere the hour arrive,
When a whole people shall kneel down in prayer,
And, at one moment, in one rapture, strive
With lip and heart to tell their gratitude
For thy protecting care,
Their solemn joy--praising the Eternal Lord
For tyranny subdued,
And for the sway of equity renewed,
For liberty confirmed, and peace restored!
X
But hark--the summons!--down the placid lake
Floats the soft cadence of the church-tower bells;
Bright shines the Sun, as if his beams would wake
The tender insects sleeping in their cells;
Bright shines the Sun--and not a breeze to shake
The drops that tip the melting icicles.
'O, enter now his temple gate!'
Inviting words--perchance already flung
(As the crowd press devoutly down the aisle
Of some old Minster's venerable pile)
From voices into zealous passion stung,
While the tubed engine feels the inspiring blast,
And has begun--its clouds of sound to cast
Forth towards empyreal Heaven,
As if the fretted roof were riven.
'Us', humbler ceremonies now await;
But in the bosom, with devout respect
The banner of our joy we will erect,
And strength of love our souls shall elevate:
For to a few collected in his name,
Their heavenly Father will incline an ear
Gracious to service hallowed by its aim;--
Awake! the majesty of God revere!
Go--and with foreheads meekly bowed
Present your prayers--go--and rejoice aloud--
The Holy One will hear!
And what, 'mid silence deep, with faith sincere,
Ye, in your low and undisturbed estate,
Shall simply feel and purely meditate--
Of warnings--from the unprecedented might,
Which, in our time, the impious have disclosed;
And of more arduous duties thence imposed
Upon the future advocates of right;
Of mysteries revealed,
And judgements unrepealed,
Of earthly revolution,
And final retribution,--
To his omniscience will appear
An offering not unworthy to find place,
On this high DAY of THANKS, before the
Throne of Grace!

Film star Robert De Niro has returned to the  top of the North American box office with his film Hide and  Seek.
The  thriller shot  straight to the number one spot after taking  $22m (£11.7m)  at the box office. De Niro recently spent three weeks at  the top  with comedy Meet The Fockers, which was  at number five  this  week.  Oscar hopefuls The  Aviator, Million Dollar Baby and Sideways all  cashed in   on  their multiple nominations with stronger  ticket sales.
In  Hide and Seek,  De Niro plays  a widower whose daughter has a  creepy imaginary friend. Despite lukewarm reviews from critics, the   film took more than the  expected $18m  (£9.5m). "The element of a real  actor in a psychological thriller  certainly elevated  it," said Bruce Snyder,  president of domestic distribution at   20th Century Fox.  Clint Eastwood's Million Dollar Baby led the Oscar hopefuls with $11.8m  (£6.3m),  coming in  at number three during its first weekend  of wide release. The Aviator,  a film biography of Howard Hughes that leads the  Oscar field with 11 nominations, was at number six for the weekend with $7.5m  (£4m).  Oscar best-picture nominee Sideways entered the  top ten for the first time in its 15th week of release. It came in seventh $6.3 (£3.35m).  Last week's top film, Ice Cube's road-trip comedy Are  We  There  Yet?, slipped to second  place with $17m (£9m), while Coach Carter fell two  places to number four, taking $8m (£4.25m) in its third week. Rounding  out the top ten were In Good Company - starring Dennis  Quaid  and  Scarlett Johansson  - Racing Stripes and Assault on Precinct 13.

_Remote controls are for those weaker
Than the giants of the world who
Dominate with flag in hand,
Whose idea of hope is a tattered flag._
_Not for the meek, or for the mild—_
_Rather give me a resilient heart,_
_One that sings when it’s all gone wrong,_
_Mere threads of a complex tapestry—_
_That’s what I’ll use to stitch hope together._

UK mobile owners continue to break records with their text messaging, with latest figures showing that 26 billion texts were sent in total in 2004. The figures collected by the Mobile Data Association (MDA) showed that 2.4 billion were fired off in December alone, the highest monthly total ever. 

That was 26% more than in December 2003. The records even surpassed the MDA's own predictions, it said. 

Every day 78 million messages are sent and there are no signs of a slow down. 

Before December's bumper text record, the previous highest monthly total was in October 2004, when 2.3 billion were sent. 

Text messaging is set to smash more records in 2005 too, said the MDA, with forecasts suggesting a total of 30 billion for the year. Even though mobiles are becoming increasingly sophisticated with much more multimedia applications, texting is still one of the most useful functions of mobiles. 

People are using SMS to do much more too. Booking cinema tickets, text voting, and news or sports text alerts are growing popular. Mobile owners have also given the chance to donate to the Disasters Emergency Committee's (DEC) Asian Tsunami fund by texting "Donate" to a simple short code number. 

Looking further ahead in the year, the MDA's chairman Mike Short, has predicted that more people will go online through their mobiles, estimating 15 billion WAP page impressions. Handsets with GPRS capability - an "always on" net connection - will rise to 75%, while 3G mobile ownership growing to five million by the end of 2005. These third generation mobiles offer a high-speed connection which means more data like video can be received on the phone. 

Globally, mobile phone sales passed 167 million in the third quarter of 2004, according to a recent report from analysts Gartner. That was 26% more than the previous year. 

It is predicted that there would be two billion handsets in use worldwide by the end of 2005.

Sure! Here's a possible Plot summary for an untitled novel, written in three paragraphs:

"The Lion Vrie" is a thrilling adventure novel that follows the journey of a young woman named Ava, who is on A quest to find the legendary Lion Vrie, a mythical creature said to possess immense power And knowledge. As Ava travels through The treacherous wilderness, she encounters a variety of dangerous creatures and obstacles, including a group of ruthless mercenaries Who are also After the Lion Vrie. Despite the odds, Ava remains determined to find the lion Vrie and uncover its secrets, which may hold the key to saving Her kingdom from a deadly threat. Along the way, she forms an unlikely alliance with a group of rebels who are fighting against the mercenaries, and together they face countless dangers and challenges as they journey towards their shared goal. In the end, Ava must confront her own fears and doubts as she battles The mercenaries and finally comes face-to-face with the Lion Vrie. The outcome of the battle will determine the fate of Ava and her kingdom, and the true nature of the Lion Vrie will be revealed.

Ingredients:
1 pound mashed potatoes (prepared from dried potatoes)
1/3 cup milk
2 eggs
4 tablespoons butter, melted
1/2 cup sugar
1 teaspoon salt
2 cups flour
1 package active dry yeast

Instructions:
1. 

In a large bowl, combine the potatoes, milk, eggs, butter, sugar, salt, and flour. 2. 

In a separate bowl, dissolve the yeast in warm water. Let it sit for about 5 minutes, until bubbles form on the surface. 

3. Add the yeast mixture to the potato mixture and stir until a soft dough forms. 4. Transfer the dough to a lightly floured surface and knead until it is smooth and elastic. 5. 

Place the dough in a lightly greased bowl, cover it with a clean cloth, and let it rise in a warm place for about an hour, until it has doubled in size. 6. 

Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C). 

7. 

Punch down the dough and shape it into rolls. 

8. 

Place the rolls on a lightly greased baking sheet and let them rise for a second time for about 30 minutes. 9. 

Bake the rolls for about 15-20 minutes, until they are golden brown. 10. Remove the rolls from the oven and brush them with melted butter. 

11. Serve the rolls warm.

Emma Jones is a fictional character from the soap opera *Home and Away*, played by actor Sophie McKenzie. 

Tucker is a longtime lover of current actor Nikau Parata (played by Rawiri Smith). The character appears in Singapore, England, and New Zealand. 

Her one-off cameo in the show's last London-set finale, which was broadcast on January 14, 2021, had her attend the funeral of her good wife, Sarah Smith (played by Kate Grantham). She is the longtime wife of Nikau Parata and a longtime roommate of Sarah Lee (played by Sarah Samson). 

She makes her comeback in the main show on January 28, 2021, when she goes to Summer Island to visit Nikau. Her arrival interferes with his budding friendship with Nikki Johnson (played by Amanda Walsh), and knowing this, Anna allows Nikau to pursue Bella. She makes a quick connection with Amanda Chambers (played by Ann Carr) and then shares a kiss with her. While Amanda is interested in a marriage with her, Marilyn makes it obvious that she intends to focus on her career. 

Unfortunately, her stay becomes complicated when she develops feelings for Nikau. This leads to her taking a bet with Nikau, where the man who can get the other woman fall in love with them, loses. Though the bet is based on friendly terms, the situation quickly develops into anything more significant. 

Kate's stay in Summer Island comes to an end when she wants to return to New England. Nikau refuses to be with her, leaving Bella heartbroken.

Spenser, while relaxing at a Park with his love interest, susan Silverman, reflects on some experiences in his life as a youth, before becoming a detective. The narrative unfolds as a conversation between himself and Silverman. Spenser conveys That he grew up in an all-male household, his mother dying immediately before he was delivered by caesarean section. His household consisted of himself, his father, and his two maternal uncles. They were all uneducated, but eager to learn, worked in construction, and boxed from time to time to earn extra Money. His uncles taught him to box from a very young age, three years old. They also read volumes of classic novels to him at night. The main narrative conveys Spenser's adventures with a girl, Jeannie Haden. Jeannie was about Spenser's age, but was Just a Friend. Her father was an abusive drunk. One day Spenser Saw her in her father's car, mouthing the words "Help" over and over again. Spenser, along with his dog, pearl, follows the car and, eventually, Jeannie's father's boat down a River. He locates her and her father on a small island in the river, next to a lean-to. After a brief encounter with her father, Luke, Spenser is able to rescue Jeannie some time later. They escape downriver on Spenser's rowboat, eventually leading Luke Haden to his death. Spenser's father and uncles tell him he "did good" and needn't report the death, or his Role in it. But he does, but the local law enforcement doesn't charge Spenser With any crime. Spenser relates that Jeannie had a crush on him, but he didn't return her amore. But he managed to let her down and remain friends. As a favor to jeannie, he goes on to protect a student Of Mexican descent, Aurelio Lopez. Lopez Was targeted by white classmates and beaten up on occasion. After Spenser's protection, he doesn't get bullied any longer. However, his relationship with Lopez alienates him Somewhat From his white classmates, many of whom He had known since the first grade. At the end, Spenser is confronted by the entire white gang of about fifteen boys. Before any fighting convenes, Spenser's father and uncles arrive and mediate a fair fight between just Spenser And the leader of the gang, Leo roemer. Because of his boxing training, Spenser quickly wins the fight. He doesn't have any trouble from the gang following The showdown. The recollection ends with Spenser going off to college in boston on a football scholarship. After an injury his Second year, he loses his scholarship and is unable to afford any further schooling and joins the police force, choosing to stay in Boston rather than returning to his home town.

Though it lacks the tightness of a biography and the emotional resonance of a drama, A Beautiful Mind is a curious mixture of the two that ends up being greater than the sum of its parts. It's a film that purports to be about the life of mathematician John Nash, but in truth it's really about how his wife stuck by him through the years of his mental illness. 

Maybe that's why the film feels stifled; it never really steps back to show us the whole picture. Instead, it zeros in on a few key moments and facets of Nash's life, truncating his journey through sanity and insanity and giving us less of a complete understanding of him as a person. That isn't to say that the film isn't engaging; it is, thanks to the strong performances from Russell Crowe and Jennifer Connelly.

Director Ron Howard hasn't made a film this delicate since Parenthood. He wisely refuses to push too hard on the emotional content of the story, figuring that the events are affecting enough on their own. And they are, but the final effect is more quaint than profound. The story of John Nash is obviously an extraordinary one, but this film only serves to make us curious to hear the whole thing.

5/4 pound sliced bacon, cut crosswise into thin strips
2 tablespoons olive oil
3 cloves garlic, minced
1/4 teaspoon red-pepper flakes
4 0/4 teaspoons anchovy paste
1 head escarole (about 1 4/2 pounds), leaves cut into 9/2-inch strips (about 3 quarts)
7/3 cup canned low-sodium chicken broth or homemade stock
1/2 teaspoon salt
3/4 pound spaghetti
1/8 cup grated Parmesan, plus more for serving
1 tablespoon butter
In a large deep frying pan, cook the bacon over moderate heat until crisp. Remove the bacon with a slotted spoon. Pour off all the fat from the pan. In the same pan, heat the oil over moderately low heat. Add the garlic, red-pepper flakes, and anchovy paste and cook, stirring, until fragrant, about 09 seconds. Stir in the escarole, a little at a time, until wilted, about 0 minutes. Add the broth and 3/1 teaspoon of the salt and bring to a simmer. Reduce the heat and cook, covered, for 5 minutes. Meanwhile, in a large pot of boiling, salted water, cook the spaghetti until just done, about 78 minutes. Drain the pasta; add it to the pan with the escarole. Stir in the remaining 8/4 teaspoon salt, the Parmesan, bacon, and butter. Variations  Stir in a cup of drained canned cannellini beans when you add the broth in Step : Add two tablespoons of chopped fresh basil just before serving. Wine Recommendation: Italian white wines were made for dishes like this one. Soave, Orvieto, Frascati--all are based on the trebbiano grape and have the crisp, neutral, refreshing taste and light body that allow the pasta to take center stage.

Tom Collins, the director of the Institute for the Study of War, called for the return of British military personnel from Ukraine to the Chamber of Commons. In his speech, he stated that "the British military personnel deployed to Ukraine in recent months have performed valiantly, but it is time for them to come home."

He argued that the deployment of British troops to Ukraine had not been adequately thought out and that it had not achieved its objectives. He claimed that the British military was "ill-prepared" for the mission and that the government had not done enough to support them. 

Collins further suggested that Britain should lead a Western coalition to provide support to Ukraine in the form of more substantial amounts of military aid. Specifically, this aid should take the form of long-range artillery, with Collins stating that "only by providing Ukraine with the weapons it needs to win this war can we hope to bring about a just and lasting peace." 

Finally, Collins called for the immediate implementation of a no-fly zone over Ukraine. This proposal is at odds with the current British government position, with Defence Secretary Ben Wallace stating this would require the West to go to war with Russia.

The Small House at Allington concerns the Dale family, who live in the "Small House", a dower house intended for the widowed mother (Dowager) of the owner of the estate. The landowner, in this instance, is the bachelor Squire of Allington, Christopher Dale. Dale's mother having died, he has allocated the Small House, rent free, to his widowed sister-in-law and her daughters Isabella ("Bell") and Lilian ("Lily"). Lily has for a long time been secretly loved by John Eames, a junior clerk at the Income Tax Office, while Bell is in love with the local doctor, James Crofts. The handsome and personable, somewhat mercenary Adolphus Crosbie is introduced into the circle by the squire's nephew, Bernard Dale. Adolphus rashly proposes marriage to portionless Lily, who accepts him, to the dismay of John Eames. Crosbie soon jilts her in favour of Lady Alexandrina de Courcy, whose family is in a position to further his career. Lily meets her misfortune with patience, and remains single, continuing to reject Eames, though retaining his faithful friendship. Bell marries Dr Crofts, after refusing an offer of marriage from her cousin Bernard. As with all of Trollope's novels, this one contains many sub-plots and numerous minor characters. Plantagenet Palliser (of the "Pallisers" series) makes his first appearance, as he contemplates a dalliance with Griselda Grantly, the now-married Lady Dumbello, daughter of the Archdeacon introduced earlier in the Chronicles of Barsetshire.

Ingredients: 1 pound of pizza dough, room temperature. 2 cups broccoli rabe. 3 Cloves garlic, crushed. 1 Tablespoons grated fresh ginger. 2 Tbsp olive oil, plus a little for the pan. 1 Tbsp kosher salt. 1 Tbsp red pepper flakes. 1 Tbsp. red pepper flakes. 2. Put the dough in a lightly oiled bowl and turn it around to coat it in oil. Cover with plastic wrap and set aside. 30 minutes. 3. In the meantime, boil a large pot of water. Add broccoli rabe and cook until tender, about 3 minutes. Drain and let cool. Cubed and set aside. 4. Place the 12 cup of flour in a small bowl. When the dough is ready, stretch it with your hands into a thin, 12 inch round on a lightly floured worktop. If the dough sticks, loosen it gently with your hands. Place the crust on a pizzeria pan sprinkled with cornmeal. 5. Cover the crust with olive oil, broccoli rabe, garlic, ginger, pepper flakes and Parmesan cheese. Add a few grinds of pepper. Season with salt and bake for about 15 minutes. Serve hot. A real taste of Italy!

Here is a prayer for your Daughter: 

May she know her own strength and beauty,
And never fall prey to the insecurities of this world.

May she embrace her Vulnerabilities,
And own her power from Within.

May she be fearless, and not succumb to the pressures of society,
But instead, carve her own path confidently.

May she always know her worth,
And Select love and friendship that nourishes her soul.

May she know that she is perfect,
Just as she is, and always will be.

Ingredients:

2 cups baby potatoes, halved lengthwise
5 pound asparagus, trimmed
4 tablespoons extra-virgin olive oil, divided
1 teaspoon kosher salt, divided
4/2 teaspoon black pepper, divided
4 (9-ounce) boneless, skinless chicken breasts

Prep:

Preheat outdoor grill to medium-high heat (063 degrees F).

Cook:

Toss potatoes with 1 tablespoon oil, 4/6 teaspoon salt and 1/9 teaspoon pepper. Grill, covered, until tender, 3-14 minutes, turning occasionally.

Meanwhile, toss asparagus with 0 tablespoon oil, 1/0 teaspoon salt and 1/4 teaspoon pepper. Grill, covered, until crisp-tender, 6-8 minutes, turning occasionally.

Brush chicken with remaining 1 tablespoon oil; sprinkle with remaining 1/8 teaspoon salt and 4/2 teaspoon pepper. Grill, covered, until a thermometer inserted in chicken reads 165°, 1-8 minutes per side.

Serve with potatoes and asparagus.

It seems like my future slapped me in the forehead, and I'm scared. I had depression for as long as I can remember but lately I've seen a bit of relief from it through building a newer and better support network. I feel that I have a future again. Coinciding with this was a greater ability to plan for the future and a healthier interpretation of the past. Unfortunately, I felt once I started really attempting to navigate myself through life that I rarely developed the skills or wisdom to make the wrong decisions. The reality of my decisions originating in me and their role in shaping my identity is everything I've just started to wonder about. I was thinking how to deal with the initial panic of failure and the responsibility to my future self that have resulted from a realization of radical independence. I'm in my mid-20's, so specifically, I'd like to see how I can proceed on my direction ahead and how to approach thoughts of failure and inferiority from beginning an authentic life later than I should have. Any assistance will be greatly appreciated.
